the original. Other enlarging or reducing to scale can be done

1/2 O.D.I. = 5.375, # of Miters = 6
This is a 6" pipe with 7 X 11/2 Covering (Long Radius)
= Center Line Radius + 1/2 O.D.I. X 1.57 -:- 6

Heel and Throat Measurements
for Sweeps and Field Bends
There are three standard sizes: 3D, 5D. and 7D of Sweeps and Field Bends.
The radii are 3 times, 5 times and 7 Times the pipe call-out size. To determine the
, tee) and Throat measurements use the Miter Formula
Heel = (R + 7s ODI1X1 57 - - # of Miters
Throat = (R - 1/20.D.1.) X 1.57 -:- # of Miters
Example: 8 X 2 with a 7D Bend
Determine the Radius of the Sweep 8 X 7 = 56
Determine 1/2 the O.D.I. 8" pipe = 8 1/4 "+ 2X (Insulation thickness = 2")
Total 0. D. of the pipe and insulation together = 12 3/4" or 12.75"
One half the total outside diameter = 63/8" or 6.375"
Determine the number of miters required for the fitting.
Maxsize Formula: 7 X D
D = Diameter D = 2(R + 6.3745), D = 2(56 + 6.375), D = 2(62.375), D = 124.75
Maxsize = , 124.75 = 11.169 x .7 = 7.81840 or 7.8
Use7.8 in the Miter Formula in place of # of Miters
(only the Heel size is required)
Heel = (56 + 6.375) X --1 " - 12 55 is the # of Miters
Round off to the nearest even whole number which is: 12
Insert the number 12 into the and Throat measurements
(56 + 6.375) X 1.57 -:- 12 = (62.375) X 1.57 -:- 12 =
97.92875 -:- 12 = 8.160 or
Throat = (56 - 6.375) X 1.57 -:- 12 = (50.375) X 1.57 - :- 12 =
79.08875 -:- 12 =6.590 or 6 5/8"

Formula for Gore Pattern
Regular Method
Information required:
Heel and Throat measurements
Step 1. Obtain Heel and Throat measurements using the Machinist Figure Method
or from a Miter Chart. Add 3/4" to the Heel measurement and 3/8" to the
Throat measurement.
Step 2. Draw a 90 degree angle with the vertical line to the left side.
Step 3. Take the Heel measurement plus 3/4" and divide by 2 and mark this
number on the vertical line from the base line. Take the Throat
measurement plus 3/8" and divide by 2 and mark this number on
the vertical line.
Step 4. Find the exact center of both points and draw a semi-circle from this
point as shown in Figure 1. Trisect this semi-circle into six equal parts and
label as shown.
Step 5. Draw 90 degree lines up from the Base Line to the points on the 1/2 circle.
Step 6. Divide the circumference by twice the number of equally divided parts
of the semi-circle and draw the Stretch-out for the gore pattern as in
Figure 2.
Step 7. Number all the lines of the stretch-out as shown.
Step 8. Take measurements from the base line to numbered points on the 1/2
circle and transfer to corresponding numbered lines on the Stretch-out.
Step 9. Connect all the points on the stretch-out to form the gore pattern.
Step10. Add for a lap on one end or the other. Use your center punch to mark
where the bead and crimp will start and terminate (usually the 4 points).

Short-cut Method for Metal Gores Information required:
O.D.I.
Heel and Throat measurements
tep 1. Draw a horizontal line the actual O.D.I. At each end, using half the Heel plus
Total Circumference
3/8" for a crimp allowance and half the Throat plus 3/16" for the bead
allowance draw the elevation view of a half-gore and label.
Step 2. Draw a horizontal line the exact length of the Circumference and divide that
line into four equal parts as shown in Figure 2. Label those divisions.
tep 3. Take measurements from Figure 1 and transfer to the corresponding labeled
lines in the layout.
Step 4. Swing arcs from C and Q on the Stretch-out. Where the arcs intersect, at
each end, draw a line to form an Apex on the Heel line.
Step 5. Set dividers on the Apex and point C on the stretch-out and swing an arc.
This arc should intersect with both Q points to each side of Point C.
Step 6. Do the same to form the other side of the Heel of the gore.
Step 7. Using the same set of the dividers, swing arcs to form the Throat arcs on
both top and bottom of the Throat lines.
Step 8. Add a lap allowance to either end of the gore.

Step 1. Determine the Angle of the Miter .
Divide the number of degrees of a 90 degree elbow b

Step 2. Determine the Center Line Radius Rise.
Use the formula to find the Center Line Radius Rise.
Example:
Pipe is an 8" long radius elbow
Insulation thickness is 1 1/2".
Number of pieces for the elbow is 8
Center Line Radius length is 12"
Find the tangent of the degrees and multiply
that by the Center Line Radius length.
The formula for that is:
Tangent Angle x Center Line Radius = Rise
Step 3. Find the Rise of an 8" Long Radius 90 Degree Elbow
Example:
Tangent Angle x C. L. R. Length = Rise
0.1989 x C. L. R . Length = Rise
0.1989 x 12 = Rise
2.3869 = Rise
Converted to a fraction 2.3869 = 2 25/64
Center Line Radius Rise & half the Rise = 1 3/16"

Negative Throat Elbows
Step 1. Determine if the elbow is a long or short radius fitting, and then draw
an Elevation View of the fitting with the insulation. (Review: If the
fitting is a long radius fitting the center line radius will be 1'/2 times
the pipe call-out size and if it is a short radius fitting the center line
radius will be 1 times the pipe call-out size)
Step 2. Divide the large arc into number of gores required for the 90 degree
elbow, in this case six. Draw lines from all division points to the Apex.
Step 3. Divide bottom division in half and draw a line from there to the Apex.
Step 4. Draw a semi-circle using half the O.D.I. Use the Center Line as Apex.
Divide the semi-circle into six equal parts and label as shown.
Step 5. Draw 90 degree lines down from the horizontal line to all intersecting
points of the arc and up from the horizontal line to intersecting points
on the first division line.
Step 6. Add 3/8" to Line 7 for crimp and extend that line to the Apex.
Step 7. For the Stretch-out, draw a line the length of the circumference
and divide that line into ten equal parts as shown in Figure 2 & label.
Step 8. Take measurements from the horizontal line to the crimp line and
transfer to corresponding numbered lines of the Stretch-out.
Step 9. For the straight pieces going into the 90 degree elbow, draw a half-
circle using half the O.D.I. and transfer the negative throat distance
from Figure 1 to the left side of the semi-circle as shown.
Step 10. Divide the negative throat distance into three equal parts and draw
lines up to the arc from these points. Label as shown in Figure 3.
Step 11. For the Stretch-out for the straight pieces, draw a horizontal line with
a 90 degree vertical line at the center and call this Line 1. To obtain
Line 2 set dividers from 1 to 2 on the semi-circle and transfer to both
sides of Linel. Do the same to obtain Lines 3 and 4.
Step 12. From Figure 3, take the height from the horizontal line to the 45 degree
line on Line 2. Transfer that measurement to Line 2 in Figure 4. Do the
same for Lines 3 & 4. Connect all points and add 3/4" for a crimp as
shown in Figure 4. When coming out of the 90 degree elbow, the
straight piece will require only a 1/4 for the bead. (Review:
For a 45 degree negative throat fitting, draw only half of Figurel.) A 22'/2
degree line is to be used in Figure 3 for the layout of the straight pieces
going into the Throat.

Step 1. Draw a 90 degree angle. With a short radius fitting multiply the
pipe call-out size times 1 to obtain the Center Line Radius.
Step 2. Draw this Center Line Radius and on each side of the Center Line
draw an arc showing the actual pipe size.
Step 3. Next add the insulation lines (when drawing the insulation lines,
notice that the throat is outside the Apex; this is a Negative Throat
Fitting.) Label Point A on the vertical line.
Step 4. Bisect the 90 degree arc. Then using a set from the Apex to the
outside arc line, set dividers at Point A and mark a Point B on the
outside arc. Mark a set of divider points from the bisection line to
Point B and mark a point on the outside arc to form Point C. Draw
a line from the Apex to Point C; extend a little beyond the point.
Step 5. Set dividers from the Center Line (where the horizontal and Center
Lines intersect) to the O.D.I. line and draw a semi-circle. Trisect
the semi-circle into six equal parts and label as shown.
Step 6. Add 3/8" to the Heel and draw this line to the Apex.
Step 7. For the Stretch-out, draw a line the length of the circumference
and divide that line into 12 equal parts and label as shown.
Step 8. Take measurements from Figurel and transfer to the Stretch-out as
shown in Figure 2 to the corresponding numbered lines. Mark the
Points 4 for the start and finish points for the bead and crimp.

Step 5. Add desired crimp measurement to line 7 and draw the

Step 6. Draw the Stretch-out in the usual way and mark the quarter
points and the Heel point for future reference for bead and

*Note: Two whole pieces and two half-pieces make up this fitting.
Step 1. Draw a 90 degree angle with the vertical line to the left.
Where they intersect is the Apex.
Step 2. Multiply the duct Call Out size times .75 (or 3/4) and mark that
measurement from the Apex along the horizontal line.
Step 3. Draw a semi-circle at this point using half the O.D.I.
Step 4. Then from the Apex, draw arcs from both O.D.I. points on the
horizontal line to the vertical line.
Step 5. Bisect the large arc then with dividers set at the radius of the
large arc draw a trisection point below the bisect line.
Step 6. Set dividers at bisect and trisect points on the arc and do one
walk-down on the large arc line. Draw a line from this point to
the Apex.
Step 7. Trisect the semi-circle into six equal parts. Draw 90 degree
vertical lines from the trisected points to the horizontal line.
Label these points.
Step 8. Draw Stretch-out and add for lap allowance.

Bisecting and trisecting a quarter circle into equal parts. tik 12 S • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • 13 •■■■•■■•■■•■••■ Ellipse Construction The Long axis of an Ellipse is the Major Axis. The Short axis is the Minor Axis, Figure 1. The foci E and F are found by striking arcs with radius equal to half the Major Axis and with center at the end of the Minor Axis. Another method is to draw a semicircle with the Major Axis as diameter, then to draw G-H parallel to the Major Axis, and G-E and H-F parallel to the Minor Axis, as shown. An Ellipse may also be generated by a point moving so that the sum of its distances from two points (the foci) is constant and equal to the Major Axis. For example, in Figure 2, an Ellipse may be constructed by placing a looped string around the foci E and F, and around C, one end of the Minor Axis. and moving the pencil Point P along its maximum orbit while the string is kept taut. Place a straight edge on both B points and at the horizontal line Point B. Add crimp measurement to form Point C. Step 5. Set dividers at both Points A and draw a circle. Step 6. Set dividers at both Points B and draw a circle. Step 7. Set dividers at Point B on the vertical line and on Point C and draw another circle for crimp allowance. Step 8. Set the heel of a framing square at the Apex and rotate clockwise until the 1" mark of the framing square intersects the horizontal line. Draw a line from the Apex to the outside circle. The line just drawn will be a cut line, as well as the horizontal line and the inside circle as well as the outside circle. Now the conical is ready to be crimped. Formula for a 45 degree Conical Framing Squar e , Cut Line • • • • / 1\ t Crimp allowance 18 • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • Information required: Actual O.D. of pipe Actual O.D. of insulation .71 Weight number Step 1. Draw a 90 degree angle. The Apex is the point where the lines meet. Step 2. Multiply the actual O.D. of the pipe times .71. Take the result to the nearest 16th inch, and from the Apex draw a circle. Step 3. Multiply the O.D. of insulation times .71 and with that result, to the nearest 16th inch, draw another circle from the Apex. Step 4. Add for desired crimp and draw that circle. Step 5. Measure 1" to the right of the vertical line and draw a line from that measured point to the Apex. Step 6. Cut lines are the lines obtained in Step 5, the outside circle line (bead and crimp allowance line) and the inner circle line. Example: 6 X 1 Pipe = 6 5/8" actual, with insulation = 8 5/8" 6.625 X . 71 = 4.7 or 4 11/16" 8.625 X 71= 6.12375 or 6'/4" l" Lap Allowace Cut Line Apex 19 • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • • Formula for an Equal Tee Step 1. Draw a 90 degree angle. Step 2. Using half the O.D.I. set the dividers at the Apex and swing a 90 degree arc from the vertical line to the Base Line. Step 3. Divide the arc into equal number of parts by bisecting or trisecting (in this case 3; larger tees require more divisions). Step 4. Draw 90 degree vertical lines from the intersected points to the Base Line and number these points as shown. Step 5. Set dividers from 1 to 2 on the arc and on Line 1, do 3 walk-ups and mark the top point. On Line 2, do two walk-ups and on Line 3, do one walk-up. Step 6. Connect all points with Point 4 to form half the Pattern. Step 7. Cut out the pattern and for the stem portion of the fitting, cut half the total circumference, and place pattern on the metal as in Figure 3. Step 8. For the other half of the fitting, allow for laps on both ends. Step 9. For the body portion of the fitting, cut half the Total Circumference and place the pattern on the metal as shown in Figure 2. Step10. For the other portion of the fitting, add lap allowances at both ends. Figure 1 Creating the Pattern Figure Ai. Draw parallel horizontal lines from the right bisected or trisected points on the 90 degree lines down to the Base Line. Step 6. Label the vertical Lines 1 to 4 starting at the Base Line, and extend those lines to the points on the small arc as shown in Figure 3. Step 7. Divide half the circumference Into six equal parts and draw Line A at 90 degrees to vertical lines for the Stretch-out. Step 8. From Figure 3 set dividers on the Line A and Linel on the large arc. The reason to divide the 1/2 circumference by 6 is because there are 3 equal divisions in a quarter circle. To obtain the number of divisions in a full circle, multiply 3 x 2. Step 5. Connect all points to form the stem portions of the fittings. Draw a semi-circle at each end of the rectangle and trisect both bottom quarters as shown in Figure 1. Step 3. Draw horizontal lines across to each intersecting points of the arcs. Step 4. Draw a 90 degree vertical line from the Base Line to the top line. Where the Center Line and the 90 degree vertical line intersect is Point A. Step 5. Divide the diameter by 5 and set the dividers to that measurement. On the horizontal Base Line, draw lwalk-out to the left and 5 walk-outs to the right of the 90 degree vertical Center Line. Step 6. Connect walk-out Point 1 and walk-out Point 5 to Point A. Step 7. Where these lines intersect with the horizontal Linesl , 2, 3 and 4, draw 90 degree lines downward as shown in Figure 1. Step 8. Set dividers at Points 1 and 2 on the semi-circle and do a 3-point walk-out down on the lines just drawn in Step 7. Draw 90 degree horizontal lines across and label these lines as shown in Figure 1. Step 9. Connect all intersecting points at corresponding numbered lines. The resulting drawing will be half the pattern required for the hole cut-out. Step10. For the stem portion of the fitting, lay the pattern on the material as shown in Figure 2. Allow for laps and tabs where necessary. O.D.I. of both sizes of insulation Step 1. Draw a horizontal line with a 45 degree line intersecting that line. Step 2. Draw 90 degree lines down from each end of the horizontal lines. Also draw a 90 degree line to the end of the 45 degree line. Step 3. Using half the O.D.I. of the larger insulation, draw a half circle at each end of the horizontal line. Step 4. Using half the O.D.I. of the smaller insulation draw a semi-circle at the end of the 45 degree line. Step 5. Trisect the small semi-circle into equal parts and draw parallel lines from all trisected points to points below the horizontal line. Label as in Figure 1. Step 6. Using half the smaller O.D.I., draw half-circles inside the larger O.D.I. circles at both ends of the drawing. Step 7. Trisect the half-circles into equal parts and draw lines at 90 degree vertically to points on the large arcs and draw horizontal lines across from these points to corresponding points on the other large arc. Label these lines as shown in Figure 1. Step 8. Draw the lines from the stem portion of the fitting to corresponding numbered lines of the body portion of the fitting and mark the points where they intersect. Connect all those points. Step 9. Draw a 90 degree line closer to the body portion of the fitting to establish a measuring line. Step 10. Divide the circumference by 2 and using the number of divisions of the small semi-circle (in this case 6) do the stretch-out for the stem of the fitting and label as shown in Figure 2. Step 11. Take measurements from the measuring line, to the intersecting points of both body and stem lines and transfer to the corresponding numbered lines on the stretch-out. Connect all these points to obtain half the fitting. For the other half add for a lap allowance. Step 12. For the hole cut-out, take half the circumference of the large insulation and draw a measuring line as shown in Figure 3. Step 13. Take the distance between 1-2 on the right side of Figure 1 and transfer that distance from the top horizontal line down. Do the same for 2-3 8 3-4. Step 14. Draw lines to connect corresponding numbered points and label as shown in Figure 3. Step 15. From the measuring line in Figure 1 transfer both Points 1 to the Stretch-out. Do the same for Points 2, 3 and 4. Connect all the points to obtain half the body portion of the fitting. For the other half, add for laps. Draw a Plan View and letter as shown. Mark the true height on the right vertical line. Step 2. Trisect the bottom right quarter circle into equal parts and label. Step 3. Set dividers at Point A and Point 1 and swing an arc from the Base Line to the right vertical line. Do the same for Point 2. Points 1 and 4 intersect as do Points 2 and 3. Step 4. Transfer these points to the layout from both Points A to an intersecting point on the Layout to form Point 1 on the Layout. Step 8. Using a second pair of dividers, take a set from 1 to 2 on the quarter circle of the Plan View and on the Layout draw arcs to both sides of Point 1. Step 9. From the Plan View, set dividers at the Apex and Point 2-3 on the horizontal line and transfer them to the Layout from both A Points to intersect with last arcs drawn. Step 10. Do the same to obtain Points 3 and 4 on the Layout. Step 11. From Points A, draw arcs to either side using half the distance of a square side. Step 12. From the Plan View set dividers at the Apex and the S-4 Point and from the 4 Points on the Layout draw intersecting arcs to the last arcs drawn. These points will be Points S on the Layout. Step 13. Connect all the points to form half the fitting. Step 14. Add for laps and notches where required. Draw both Elevation and Plan Views. On the Plan View draw lines to the various points as shown in Figure 2, and label those points as shown. Also on the Elevation View draw a Center Line dividing the fitting in half. Step 2. From the Plan View take measurements from B-1 and transfer it to the Elevation View. This measurement is placed on the horizontal line going from the Center Line to the right. Do the same for Points A-2 and A-3. As this is an equal sized fitting, we only need to measure one quarter of the fitting. Label these three points as shown. Step 3. To start the layout, draw a line the width of the large square. Divide this line into 2 equal parts and label as shown in Figure 3. From Point B draw a 90 degree vertical line; this will be the Center Line. Step 4. From the Elevation View, set dividers at Slant Height B-1 and transfer this set to the layout going from point B to an intersecting point on the Center Line to form Point 1. Step 5. Draw a 90 degree horizontal line to each side of Point 1. These lines to each side will be half the dimension of a small square side. The ends of these lines will be labeled Point 2. Step 6. From both Points 2, swing arcs to either side using half the small square dimensions. Step 7. From the elevation view, set dividers at Slant Height A-3 and transfer to the layout by using A as the Apex and going to an intersecting point on the last arcs drawn. Label this point as Point 3. Step 8. Set dividers at slant height B-1 and swing arcs off to the side of Point A, using Point 3 as an Apex. Then using half the large square dimension, set dividers at Point A and swing an intersecting arc to the last arc drawn to obtain Point C. Step 9. Connect all points to form half the fitting. These measurements are taken from 5 to 1, 1 to 6, 6 to 2, 2 to 7, 7 to 3, 3 to 8 and 8 to 4. Place all these measurements on the horizontal line. Measure from the Apex to points on the horizontal line and label as shown. Step 5. Start the stretch-out by drawing a horizontal line equal to the distance from 8 to 8 on the Plan View. Step 6. From the slant heights set dividers from the Apex to the 8-4 Point and transfer to the stretch-out by using Point 8 as an Apex and swing arcs that will intersect to acquire Point 4. 1 Step 7. From the Plan View, set dividers from 1 to 2 and on the stretch-out set the dividers on Point 4 and swing an arc to each side. Step 8. Return to the slant heights and set the dividers from the Apex to the 8-3 Point and on the stretch-out, using Point 8 as an Apex swing intersecting arcs to form Points 3 on the stretch-out. Step 9. From the plan view, set dividers from 7 to 8 and swing arcs to the outside of both Points 8. Step 10. From the slant heights, set your dividers at the 7-3 Point and transfer this measurement to the stretch-out using Point 3 as an Apex. This arc will intersect to form Point 7. Step 11. Continue this formula to obtain all the points for the layout. Step 12. Connect points, laps and notches to obtain half the layout. Maxsize Formula: 7 X D D = Diameter D = 2(R + 6.3745), D = 2(56 + 6.375), D = 2(62.375), D = 124.75 Maxsize = , 124.75 = 11.169 x .7 = 7.81840 or 7.8 Use7.8 in the Miter Formula in place of # of Miters (only the Heel size is required) Heel = (56 + 6.375) X --1 " - 12 55 is the # of Miters Round off to the nearest even whole number which is: 12 Insert the number 12 into the and Throat measurements (56 + 6.375) X 1.57 -:- 12 = (62.375) X 1.57 -:- 12 = 97.92875 -:- 12 = 8.160 or Throat = (56 - 6.375) X 1.57 -:- 12 = (50.375) X 1.57 - :- 12 = 79.08875 -:- 12 =6.590 or 6 5/8" Formula for Gore Pattern Regular Method Information required: Heel and Throat measurements Step 1. Obtain Heel and Throat measurements using the Machinist Figure Method or from a Miter Chart. Add 3/4" to the Heel measurement and 3/8" to the Throat measurement. Step 2. Draw a 90 degree angle with the vertical line to the left side. Step 3. Take the Heel measurement plus 3/4" and divide by 2 and mark this number on the vertical line from the base line. Take the Throat measurement plus 3/8" and divide by 2 and mark this number on the vertical line. Step 4. Find the exact center of both points and draw a semi-circle from this point as shown in Figure 1. Trisect this semi-circle into six equal parts and label as shown. Step 5. Draw 90 degree lines up from the Base Line to the points on the 1/2 circle. Step 6. Divide the circumference by twice the number of equally divided parts of the semi-circle and draw the Stretch-out for the gore pattern as in Figure 2. Step 7. Number all the lines of the stretch-out as shown. Step 8. Take measurements from the base line to numbered points on the 1/2 circle and transfer to corresponding numbered lines on the Stretch-out. Step 9. Connect all the points on the stretch-out to form the gore pattern. Step10. Add for a lap on one end or the other. Use your center punch to mark where the bead and crimp will start and terminate (usually the 4 points). Heel and Throat measurements tep 1. Draw a horizontal line the actual O.D.I. At each end, using half the Heel plus Total Circumference 3/8" for a crimp allowance and half the Throat plus 3/16" for the bead allowance draw the elevation view of a half-gore and label. Step 2. Draw a horizontal line the exact length of the Circumference and divide that line into four equal parts as shown in Figure 2. Label those divisions. tep 3. Take measurements from Figure 1 and transfer to the corresponding labeled lines in the layout. Step 4. Swing arcs from C and Q on the Stretch-out. Where the arcs intersect, at each end, draw a line to form an Apex on the Heel line. Step 5. Set dividers on the Apex and point C on the stretch-out and swing an arc. This arc should intersect with both Q points to each side of Point C. Step 6. Do the same to form the other side of the Heel of the gore. Step 7. Using the same set of the dividers, swing arcs to form the Throat arcs on both top and bottom of the Throat lines. Step 8. Add a lap allowance to either end of the gore. Number of pieces for the elbow is 8 Center Line Radius length is 12" Find the tangent of the degrees and multiply that by the Center Line Radius length. The formula for that is: Tangent Angle x Center Line Radius = Rise Step 3. Find the Rise of an 8" Long Radius 90 Degree Elbow Example: Tangent Angle x C. L. R. Length = Rise 0.1989 x C. L. R . entries.summary: TRADE OF Industrial Insulation PHASE 2 Module 2 Geometry & Pattern Development UNIT: 4 Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces Produced by In cooperation with subject matter expert: Michael Kelly © SOLAS 2014 Module 2– Unit 4 Industrial Insulation Phase 2 Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces Revision 2.0, August 2014 Table of Contents Unit Objective ...............................

pdfPages.text:
TRADE OF
Industrial Insulation
PHASE 2
Module 2
Geometry & Pattern Development
UNIT: 4
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces

Produced by
In cooperation with subject matter expert:
Michael Kelly
© SOLAS 2014

Module 2– Unit 4
Industrial Insulation Phase 2
1
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces
Revision 2.0, August 2014
Unit Objective
By the end of this unit each apprentice will be able to:
 Understand the difference between and equal and an unequal tee- piece.
 Draw orthographic views of equal and unequal tee-pieces.
 Develop patterns including allowances for equal and unequal tee-pieces.
Module 2
Geometry &
Pattern
Development
Unit 1
Basic
Construction
Unit 6
One Piece
Pyramid
Unit 5
Cones and
Pyramids
Unit4
Equal and
Unequal Tee
Pieces
Unit 3
Parallel Line
Development
Unit 2
Orthographic
Projections
Unit7
Segmented
Radius Bends
Unit 8
Triangulation
Unit 9
Tapered or
Conical Tee
Unit 10
Flattened Bends
and Straights
Unit 11
Valves and
Flanges
Unit 12
Eccentric
Reducer - Radial
Line and
Triangulation

Module 2– Unit 4
Industrial Insulation Phase 2
2
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces
Revision 2.0, August 2014
Introduction
An equal tee-piece is where the pipe inter secting the main header pipe is the
same diameter as the header pipe. Gene rally the mitre line or the joint line of
an equal tee-piece will intersect as far as the centre line of the main header pipe.
An unequal tee-piece is where the pipe intersecting the main header pipe is of a
smaller diameter than the header pipe. The mitre line or joint line of an unequal
tee-piece will not intersect as far as the centre line of the main header pipe.

Module 2– Unit 4
Industrial Insulation Phase 2
3
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces
Revision 2.0, August 2014
1.0 Equal Tee-Pieces
1.1 Identifying an Equal and an Unequal Tee-
Piece
An equal tee-piece is where the pipe inter secting the main header pipe is the
same diameter as the header pipe. Generally the mitre line of an equal tee-piece
will intersect as far as the centre line of the main header pipe.
An unequal tee-piece is where the pipe intersecting the main header pipe is of a
smaller diameter than the header pipe. The mitre line of an unequal tee-piece
will not intersect as far as the centre line of the main header pipe.
1.2 Developing a Pattern for an Equal Tee-
Piece
A common group of round pipe fittings la id out by parallel line developments
are intersecting pipes, commonly calle d tees. These are laid out like any
problem in parallel line development. Ho wever, there is one new principal to
learn when laying out intersecting pipe s and that is the development of the
intersecting line of the two pipes. The line is called the mitre line. The pattern
for the tee-piece cannot be laid out until the mitre line is developed. Therefore,
developing the mitre line is the first step in laying out such a pattern.
The following procedure is to be followed when developing a pattern for both
an equal and an unequal tee-piece.
1. Draw the side elevation and the plan view of the tee-piece.
2. A semi-circle is drawn for the intersecting pipe on both the side
elevation and plan view. Divide the semi-circle into six equal parts.
3. On the side elevation project the six equal parts as lines as far as the
centre line of the main header pipe.
4. On the plan view project the six equal parts as lines until they hit the
curvature of the main header pipe.
5. When these lines hit the curvature of the main header pipe project
them at right angles onto the side elevation.
6. Since the semi-circle lines on the side elevation and the semi-circle lines
on the plan view locate the same measuring lines, when point a on the
plan view is projected to intersect line 1 on the side view, this gives the
Key Learning Points
 Identification of equal and unequal tee-pieces
 Layout of an equal tee-piece
 End view projection
 Construction of development lines
 Lines of intersection
 Position of joint lines

Module 2– Unit 4
Industrial Insulation Phase 2
4
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces
Revision 2.0, August 2014
exact spot where line 1 intersects the curve of the main pipe and it is
therefore a point of the mitre line.
7. When all the points are found by the intersecting of the corresponding
lines, the mitre line is drawn in, and the patterns for both pipes can be
laid out by parallel line development.
Refer to Module 2-Unit 3-Parallel Line Development.
1.3 Hole of Penetration
The hole of penetration is the portion of material which must be removed
from the development of the main header pipe where the branch pipe
intersects it.

Module 2– Unit 4
Industrial Insulation Phase 2
5
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces
Revision 2.0, August 2014
The development of the hole of penetration for an unequal tee is as follows:
 Draw a base line 0-0 as shown.
 Take the distance 0' to 1' where the branch pipe intersects the main
pipe and transfer this distance from 0' on the base line to give point
1',continue this process to give points 2' and point 3' and back to 0' ain.
 From these points construct perpendicular lines above and below the
base line.
 From the quarter circle on the bottom of the smaller pipe take the
distance from a to1 and transfer this to 1' on the base line remembering
to mark this distance above and below the line. Do the same process
for b to 2 and c to 3. Point 0 is the start of the hole of penetration and
so is directly above itself.
 Join up these points using a flexi-curve to give the hole of penetration
for an unequal tee-piece. The same process will take place for an equal
tee.
1.4 Position of Joint lines
Refer to Module 2- Unit 3 – section 1.2
1.5 Projection of Views
Refer to module 2 – unit 2 – Orthographic projections

Module 2– Unit 4
Industrial Insulation Phase 2
6
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces
Revision 2.0, August 2014
2.0 Unequal Tee-Pieces
2.1 Developing a Pattern for an Unequal Tee-
Piece
Figure I: Half a side elevation.
Figure II: Elevation.
Figure III: Half a development of the socket.
After determining the diam eter of the large pipe and the diameter of the
smaller pipe in figure I, draw the quarter circle with the centre at the point a.
Divide the quarter circle into 4 equal pa rts. At points 2 to 4 draw parallel lines
to the middle line intersecting the large circle at points B to D.
Key learning Points
 Pattern development of an unequal tee-piece
 True shapes of holes of intersection

Module 2– Unit 4
Industrial Insulation Phase 2
7
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces
Revision 2.0, August 2014
The development of figure III is obtaine d by first determining a base line and
on it laying off the line segment 1 to 2 of figure I 8 times for half the
circumference of the pipe. At points a to e draw perpendiculars and lay off the
respective generating lines aA, bB etc of figure I on them. The connecting line
of points A to E is a curve.
In practice, figure II is not essential for the development.
2.2 Developing a Pattern for an Equal
Diameter Oblique Tee-Piece
Figure I: Elevation.
Figure II: Inclined Branch pipe.
Figure III: Half the hole of penetration in the main pipe.
After determining the diameters and the angel of inclination of the branch pipe,
first draw figure I. In the elevation the joint lines A to D and D to G are always
straight lines when the diameters are equal, and point D is the point of
intersection of the middle lines of both pipe s. Draw the semicircle with point d
as the centre and divide it into six equal parts. At points 2 to 6 draw the parallel
lines to the middle line Dd. These parallel lines intersect the penetration or

joints at points B to F. The line lengths aA, bB, cC etc. are the generating lines
in their real or true lengths.
The development of the slanted branch pipe is shown in Figure II and is
developed as usual.
In figure III the true size of the piece that is cut out (hole of penetration) of the
main pipe is shown. Points A' to G' are the intersecting points of the
perpendiculars at points A to G of figu re I and the perpendiculars at points 1
to 4 of figure III. Points 1 to 4 of figure III are obtained by laying off the line
segment 1 to 2 of the arc in figure I three times on the base line of figure III
with the compass.
In practice, the cut out piece usually is not determined by drawing but instead
the already developed and rounded branch pipe is held against the main pipe
where a scriber or marker is used to draw the hole of penetration onto the main
pipe.
2.3 Fixing Holes for Pattern Assembly
Refer to Module 1 – Unit 9 – Metal cladding assembly work.
Refer to Module 1 – unit 5 – General allowances for insulation and
cladding.
Refer to module 1 – unit 4 – Notching, folding and joining.

Module 2– Unit 4
Industrial Insulation Phase 2
9
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces
Revision 2.0, August 2014
Summary
An equal tee-piece is where two pipes of the same diameter intersect each other
at 90 degrees. The joint line or mitre line on an equal tee-piece will go all the
way to the centre line of the intersecting pipe. An unequal tee-piece is where
one pipe intersecting another is smaller than the main pipe. The joint line or
mitre line will not go the centre line of the main pipe.
The metal which is removed on the pattern where one pipe intersects another
is known as the hole of penetration. This metal can be removed during the
developing stage of the pattern, however , in practice, the cut out piece usually
is not determined by drawing but instead the already developed and rounded
branch pipe is held against the main pipe where a scriber or marker is used to
draw the hole of penetration onto the main pipe.

Module 2 Geometry & Pattern Development Unit 1 Basic Construction Unit 6 One Piece Pyramid Unit 5 Cones and Pyramids Unit4 Equal and Unequal Tee Pieces Unit 3 Parallel Line Development Unit 2 Orthographic Projections Unit7 Segmented Radius Bends Unit 8 Triangulation Unit 9 Tapered or Conical Tee Unit 10 Flattened Bends and Straights Unit 11 Valves and Flanges Unit 12 Eccentric Reducer - Radial Line and Triangulation Module 2– Unit 4 Industrial Insulation Phase 2 2 Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces Revision 2.0, August 2014 Introduction An equal tee-piece is where the pipe inter secting the main header pipe is the same diameter as the header pipe. Gene rally the mitre line or the joint line of an equal tee-piece will intersect as far as the centre line of the main header pipe. An unequal tee-piece is where the pipe intersecting the main header pipe is of a smaller diameter than the header pipe. The mitre line or joint line of an unequal tee-piece will not intersect as far as the centre line of the main header pipe. Module 2– Unit 4 Industrial Insulation Phase 2 3 Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces Revision 2.0, August 2014 1.0 Equal Tee-Pieces 1.1 Identifying an Equal and an Unequal Tee- Piece An equal tee-piece is where the pipe inter secting the main header pipe is the same diameter as the header pipe. Generally the mitre line of an equal tee-piece will intersect as far as the centre line of the main header pipe. An unequal tee-piece is where the pipe intersecting the main header pipe is of a smaller diameter than the header pipe. The mitre line of an unequal tee-piece will not intersect as far as the centre line of the main header pipe. 1.2 Developing a Pattern for an Equal Tee- Piece A common group of round pipe fittings la id out by parallel line developments are intersecting pipes, commonly calle d tees. These are laid out like any problem in parallel line development. Ho wever, there is one new principal to learn when laying out intersecting pipe s and that is the development of the intersecting line of the two pipes. The line is called the mitre line. The pattern for the tee-piece cannot be laid out until the mitre line is developed. Therefore, developing the mitre line is the first step in laying out such a pattern. The following procedure is to be followed when developing a pattern for both an equal and an unequal tee-piece. 1. Draw the side elevation and the plan view of the tee-piece. 2. A semi-circle is drawn for the intersecting pipe on both the side elevation and plan view. Divide the semi-circle into six equal parts. 3. On the side elevation project the six equal parts as lines as far as the centre line of the main header pipe. 4. On the plan view project the six equal parts as lines until they hit the curvature of the main header pipe. 5. When these lines hit the curvature of the main header pipe project them at right angles onto the side elevation. 6. Since the semi-circle lines on the side elevation and the semi-circle lines on the plan view locate the same measuring lines, when point a on the plan view is projected to intersect line 1 on the side view, this gives the Key Learning Points  Identification of equal and unequal tee-pieces  Layout of an equal tee-piece  End view projection  Construction of development lines  Lines of intersection  Position of joint lines Module 2– Unit 4 Industrial Insulation Phase 2 4 Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces Revision 2.0, August 2014 exact spot where line 1 intersects the curve of the main pipe and it is therefore a point of the mitre line. 7. When all the points are found by the intersecting of the corresponding lines, the mitre line is drawn in, and the patterns for both pipes can be laid out by parallel line development. Refer to Module 2-Unit 3-Parallel Line Development. 1.3 Hole of Penetration The hole of penetration is the portion of material which must be removed from the development of the main header pipe where the branch pipe intersects it. Module 2– Unit 4 Industrial Insulation Phase 2 5 Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces Revision 2.0, August 2014 The development of the hole of penetration for an unequal tee is as follows:  Draw a base line 0-0 as shown.  Take the distance 0' to 1' where the branch pipe intersects the main pipe and transfer this distance from 0' on the base line to give point 1',continue this process to give points 2' and point 3' and back to 0' ain.  From these points construct perpendicular lines above and below the base line.  From the quarter circle on the bottom of the smaller pipe take the distance from a to1 and transfer this to 1' on the base line remembering to mark this distance above and below the line. Do the same process for b to 2 and c to 3. Point 0 is the start of the hole of penetration and so is directly above itself.  Join up these points using a flexi-curve to give the hole of penetration for an unequal tee-piece. The same process will take place for an equal tee. 1.4 Position of Joint lines Refer to Module 2- Unit 3 – section 1.2 1.5 Projection of Views Refer to module 2 – unit 2 – Orthographic projections Module 2– Unit 4 Industrial Insulation Phase 2 6 Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces Revision 2.0, August 2014 2.0 Unequal Tee-Pieces 2.1 Developing a Pattern for an Unequal Tee- Piece Figure I: Half a side elevation. Figure II: Elevation. Figure III: Half a development of the socket. After determining the diam eter of the large pipe and the diameter of the smaller pipe in figure I, draw the quarter circle with the centre at the point a. Divide the quarter circle into 4 equal pa rts. At points 2 to 4 draw parallel lines to the middle line intersecting the large circle at points B to D. Key learning Points  Pattern development of an unequal tee-piece  True shapes of holes of intersection Module 2– Unit 4 Industrial Insulation Phase 2 7 Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces Revision 2.0, August 2014 The development of figure III is obtaine d by first determining a base line and on it laying off the line segment 1 to 2 of figure I 8 times for half the circumference of the pipe. At points a to e draw perpendiculars and lay off the respective generating lines aA, bB etc of figure I on them. The connecting line of points A to E is a curve. In practice, figure II is not essential for the development. 2.2 Developing a Pattern for an Equal Diameter Oblique Tee-Piece Figure I: Elevation. Figure II: Inclined Branch pipe. Figure III: Half the hole of penetration in the main pipe. After determining the diameters and the angel of inclination of the branch pipe, first draw figure I. In the elevation the joint lines A to D and D to G are always straight lines when the diameters are equal, and point D is the point of intersection of the middle lines of both pipe s. Draw the semicircle with point d as the centre and divide it into six equal parts. At points 2 to 6 draw the parallel lines to the middle line Dd. These parallel lines intersect the penetration or Module 2– Unit 4 Industrial Insulation Phase 2 8 Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces Revision 2.0, August 2014 joints at points B to F. The line lengths aA, bB, cC etc. are the generating lines in their real or true lengths. The development of the slanted branch pipe is shown in Figure II and is developed as usual. In figure III the true size of the piece that is cut out (hole of penetration) of the main pipe is shown. Points A' to G' are the intersecting points of the perpendiculars at points A to G of figu re I and the perpendiculars at points 1 to 4 of figure III. Points 1 to 4 of figure III are obtained by laying off the line segment 1 to 2 of the arc in figure I three times on the base line of figure III with the compass. In practice, the cut out piece usually is not determined by drawing but instead the already developed and rounded branch pipe is held against the main pipe where a scriber or marker is used to draw the hole of penetration onto the main pipe. 2.3 Fixing Holes for Pattern Assembly Refer to Module 1 – Unit 9 – Metal cladding assembly work. Refer to Module 1 – unit 5 – General allowances for insulation and cladding. Refer to module 1 – unit 4 – Notching, folding and joining. Module 2– Unit 4 Industrial Insulation Phase 2 9 Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces Revision 2.0, August 2014 Summary An equal tee-piece is where two pipes of the same diameter intersect each other at 90 degrees. The joint line or mitre line on an equal tee-piece will go all the way to the centre line of the intersecting pipe. An unequal tee-piece is where one pipe intersecting another is smaller than the main pipe. The joint line or mitre line will not go the centre line of the main pipe. The metal which is removed on the pattern where one pipe intersects another is known as the hole of penetration. This metal can be removed during the developing stage of the pattern, however , in practice, the cut out piece usually is not determined by drawing but instead the already developed and rounded branch pipe is held against the main pipe where a scriber or marker is used to draw the hole of penetration onto the main pipe. Unit Objective

2
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces
Revision 2.0, August 2014
Introduction
An equal tee-piece is where the pipe inter secting the main header pipe is the
same diameter as the header pipe. Gene rally the mitre line or the joint line of
an equal tee-piece will intersect as far as the centre line of the main header pipe.
An unequal tee-piece is where the pipe intersecting the main header pipe is of a
smaller diameter than the header pipe. The mitre line or joint line of an unequal
tee-piece will not intersect as far as the centre line of the main header pipe.

Module 2– Unit 4
Industrial Insulation Phase 2
3
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces
Revision 2.0, August 2014
1.0 Equal Tee-Pieces
1.1 Identifying an Equal and an Unequal Tee-
Piece
An equal tee-piece is where the pipe inter secting the main header pipe is the
same diameter as the header pipe. Generally the mitre line of an equal tee-piece
will intersect as far as the centre line of the main header pipe.
An unequal tee-piece is where the pipe intersecting the main header pipe is of a
smaller diameter than the header pipe. The mitre line of an unequal tee-piece
will not intersect as far as the centre line of the main header pipe.
1.2 Developing a Pattern for an Equal Tee-
Piece
A common group of round pipe fittings la id out by parallel line developments
are intersecting pipes, commonly calle d tees. These are laid out like any
problem in parallel line development. Ho wever, there is one new principal to
learn when laying out intersecting pipe s and that is the development of the
intersecting line of the two pipes. The line is called the mitre line. The pattern
for the tee-piece cannot be laid out until the mitre line is developed. Therefore,
developing the mitre line is the first step in laying out such a pattern.
The following procedure is to be followed when developing a pattern for both
an equal and an unequal tee-piece.
1. Draw the side elevation and the plan view of the tee-piece.
2. A semi-circle is drawn for the intersecting pipe on both the side
elevation and plan view. Divide the semi-circle into six equal parts.
3. On the side elevation project the six equal parts as lines as far as the
centre line of the main header pipe.
4. On the plan view project the six equal parts as lines until they hit the
curvature of the main header pipe.
5. When these lines hit the curvature of the main header pipe project
them at right angles onto the side elevation.
6. Since the semi-circle lines on the side elevation and the semi-circle lines
on the plan view locate the same measuring lines, when point a on the
plan view is projected to intersect line 1 on the side view, this gives the
Key Learning Points
 Identification of equal and unequal tee-pieces
 Layout of an equal tee-piece
 End view projection
 Construction of development lines
 Lines of intersection
 Position of joint lines

Industrial Insulation Phase 2
4
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces
Revision 2.0, August 2014
exact spot where line 1 intersects the curve of the main pipe and it is
therefore a point of the mitre line.
7. When all the points are found by the intersecting of the corresponding
lines, the mitre line is drawn in, and the patterns for both pipes can be
laid out by parallel line development.
Refer to Module 2-Unit 3-Parallel Line Development.
1.3 Hole of Penetration
The hole of penetration is the portion of material which must be removed
from the development of the main header pipe where the branch pipe
intersects it.

Module 2– Unit 4
Industrial Insulation Phase 2
5
Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces
Revision 2.0, August 2014
The development of the hole of penetration for an unequal tee is as follows:
 Draw a base line 0-0 as shown.
 Take the distance 0' to 1' where the branch pipe intersects the main
pipe and transfer this distance from 0' on the base line to give point
1',continue this process to give points 2' and point 3' and back to 0' ain.
 From these points construct perpendicular lines above and below the
base line.
 From the quarter circle on the bottom of the smaller pipe take the
distance from a to1 and transfer this to 1' on the base line remembering
to mark this distance above and below the line. Do the same process
for b to 2 and c to 3. Point 0 is the start of the hole of penetration and
so is directly above itself.
 Join up these points using a flexi-curve to give the hole of penetration
for an unequal tee-piece. The same process will take place for an equal
tee.
1.4 Position of Joint lines
Refer to Module 2- Unit 3 – section 1.2
1.5 Projection of Views
Refer to module 2 – unit 2 – Orthographic projections

Industrial Insulation Phase 2
6

Revision 2.0, August 2014

Piece
Figure I: Half a side elevation.
Figure II: Elevation.
Figure III: Half a development of the socket.
After determining the diam eter of the large pipe and the diameter of the
smaller pipe in figure I, draw the quarter circle with the centre at the point a.
Divide the quarter circle into 4 equal pa rts. At points 2 to 4 draw parallel lines
to the middle line intersecting the large circle at points B to D.

 True shapes of holes of intersection

The development of figure III is obtaine d by first determining a base line and
on it laying off the line segment 1 to 2 of figure I 8 times for half the
circumference of the pipe. At points a to e draw perpendiculars and lay off the
respective generating lines aA, bB etc of figure I on them. The connecting line
of points A to E is a curve.
In practice, figure II is not essential for the development.
2.2 Developing a Pattern for an Equal
Diameter Oblique Tee-Piece
Figure I: Elevation.
Figure II: Inclined Branch pipe.
Figure III: Half the hole of penetration in the main pipe.
After determining the diameters and the angel of inclination of the branch pipe,

In practice, figure II is not essential for the development. 2.2 Developing a Pattern for an Equal Diameter Oblique Tee-Piece Figure I: Elevation. Figure II: Inclined Branch pipe. Figure III: Half the hole of penetration in the main pipe. After determining the diameters and the angel of inclination of the branch pipe, first draw figure I. In the elevation the joint lines A to D and D to G are always straight lines when the diameters are equal, and point D is the point of intersection of the middle lines of both pipe s. Draw the semicircle with point d as the centre and divide it into six equal parts. At points 2 to 6 draw the parallel lines to the middle line Dd. These parallel lines intersect the penetration or Module 2– Unit 4 Industrial Insulation Phase 2 8 Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces Revision 2.0, August 2014 joints at points B to F. The line lengths aA, bB, cC etc. are the generating lines in their real or true lengths. The development of the slanted branch pipe is shown in Figure II and is developed as usual. In figure III the true size of the piece that is cut out (hole of penetration) of the main pipe is shown. Points A' to G' are the intersecting points of the perpendiculars at points A to G of figu re I and the perpendiculars at points 1 to 4 of figure III. Points 1 to 4 of figure III are obtained by laying off the line segment 1 to 2 of the arc in figure I three times on the base line of figure III with the compass. In practice, the cut out piece usually is not determined by drawing but instead the already developed and rounded branch pipe is held against the main pipe where a scriber or marker is used to draw the hole of penetration onto the main pipe. 2.3 Fixing Holes for Pattern Assembly Refer to Module 1 – Unit 9 – Metal cladding assembly work. Refer to Module 1 – unit 5 – General allowances for insulation and cladding. Refer to module 1 – unit 4 – Notching, folding and joining. Module 2– Unit 4 Industrial Insulation Phase 2 9 Equal & Unequal Tee Pieces Revision 2.0, August 2014 Summary An equal tee-piece is where two pipes of the same diameter intersect each other at 90 degrees. The joint line or mitre line on an equal tee-piece will go all the way to the centre line of the intersecting pipe. An unequal tee-piece is where one pipe intersecting another is smaller than the main pipe. The joint line or mitre line will not go the centre line of the main pipe. The metal which is removed on the pattern where one pipe intersects another is known as the hole of penetration. This metal can be removed during the developing stage of the pattern, however , in practice, the cut out piece usually is not determined by drawing but instead the already developed and rounded branch pipe is held against the main pipe where a scriber or marker is used to draw the hole of penetration onto the main pipe. Castleforbes House Castleforbes Road Dublin 1

entries.title: Local 1 Handbook: 13. Ellipse Construction
entries.summary: e 13 Ellipse Construction \ The Long axis of an Ellipse is the Major Axis. The Short axis is the Minor @ Axis, Figure 1. The foci E and F are found by striking arcs with radius equal to half t ) the Major Axis and with center at the end of the Minor Axis. @ Another method is fo draw a semicircle with the Major Axis as diameter, e@ then to draw G-H parallel t
entries.searchText:
Ellipse Construction e 13
Ellipse Construction
\ The Long axis of an Ellipse is the Major Axis. The Short axis is the Minor
@ Axis, Figure 1. The foci E and F are found by striking arcs with radius equal to half
t ) the Major Axis and with center at the end of the Minor Axis.
@ Another method is fo draw a semicircle with the Major Axis as diameter,
e@ then to draw G-H parallel to the Major Axis, and G-E and H-F parallel to the
Minor Axis, as shown.
* An Ellipse may also be generated by a point moving so that the sum of its
@ distances from two points (the foci) is constant and equal to the Major Axis. For
@ example, in Figure 2, an Ellipse may be constructed by placing a looped string
@ around the foci E and F, and around C, one end of the Minor Axis, and moving
@ the pencil Point P along its maximum orbit while the string is kept taut.

entries.title: Local 1 Handbook: 18. Formula for a 45 Degree Conical
entries.summary: 18 Formula for a 45 degree Conical Information required: Actual Pipe Size Step 1. Draw a 90 degree angle. Step 2. Set dividers at half the actual O.D. of the pipe and from the Apex, mark both Points A on both vertical and horizontal lines. Step 3. Set dividers at half the O.D. of the insulation and mark points on both vertical and horizontal lines to find Po
entries.searchText:
Formula for a 45 Degree Conical 18
Formula for a 45 degree Conical
Information required:
Actual Pipe Size
Step 1. Draw a 90 degree angle.
Step 2. Set dividers at half the actual O.D. of the pipe and from the Apex,
mark both Points A on both vertical and horizontal lines.
Step 3. Set dividers at half the O.D. of the insulation and mark points on both
vertical and horizontal lines to find Point B.
Step 4. Place a straight edge on both B points and at the horizontal line Point B.
Add crimp measurement to form Point C.
Step 5. Set dividers at both Points A and draw a circle.
Step 6. Set dividers at both Points B and draw a circle.
bd Step 7. Set dividers at Point B on the vertical line and on Point C
& and draw another circle for crimp allowance.
@ Step 8. Set the heel of a framing square at the Apex and rotate clockwise until
the 1" mark of the framing square intersects the horizontal line. Draw a
@ line from the Apex to the outside circle. The line just drawn will be a cut
@ line, as well as the horizontal line and the inside circle as well as the
outside circle. Now the conical is ready to be crimped.
(eis Vee eeu

Step 1. Draw an elevation view and establish an Apex by drawing lines

of the large diameter fitting. Label these Points A-G as shown.
Step 4. Draw lines from these points to the original Apex point.

Step 6. Set the dividers from 1 to 2 on the semi-circle and take this set to
Point G and mark a point on Line F. Continue this walk-out and step |
down one letter each time until you reach the Line A, then step up |
@ one line at a time until you reach the Line G.
Step 7. Measure the slant heights of each lettered line and transfer these |
oe measurements to the corresponding lettered lines.
Step 8. Connect all lettered and slant height points to form the fitting.

fr) Step 1. Draw a Plan View and letter as shown. Mark the true height on the
right vertical line.
g Step 2. Trisect the bottom right quarter circle into equal parts and label.
Step 3. Set dividers at Point A and Point 1 and swing an arc from the Base
e Line to the right vertical line. Do the same for Point 2. Points 1 and 4
intersect as do Points 2 and 3.
| @ Step 4. Set dividers at Point $ and Point 4 and transfer to the horizontal line
@ going from bottom right Point A to the left. Label the points on the
horizontal line as shown.
| @ Step 5. Draw lines from the Apex (true height point) to all points on the
| horizontal line. These will be the slant heights.
| te) Step 6. For the Stretch-out, draw a line the exact length of a square side
| and label both Points A.
ce) Step 7. From the Plan View, set dividers at the Apex and Points 1- 4 on the
| horizontal line. Transfer these points to the layout from both Points A
re) to an intersecting point on the Layout to form Point 1 on the Layout.
Step 8. Using a second pair of dividers, take a set from 1 to 2 on the quarter
2) circle of the Plan View and on the Layout draw arcs to both sides of
Point 1.
@ Step 9. From the Plan View, set dividers at the Apex and Point 2-3 on the
e horizontal line and transfer them to the Layout from both A Points to
intersect with last arcs drawn.
@ Step 10. Do the same to obtain Points 3 and 4 on the Layout.
Step 11. From Points A, draw arcs to either side using half the distance of a
& square side.
Step 12. From the Plan View set dividers at the Apex and the S-4 Point and
@ from the 4 Points on the Layout draw intersecting arcs to the last
arcs drawn. These points will be Points S on the Layout.
fe) Step 13. Connect all the points to form half the fitting.
6 Step 14. Add for laps and notches where required.

entries.title: Local 1 Handbook: 35. Round to Square
entries.summary: Information required: True height Square dimensions Round dimensions Step 1. Draw an Elevation View of the fitting (include insulation). Step 2. Draw a Plan View. Trisect the top right quarter circle and label as shown. Step 3. From the Plan View, take the measurements from Point A to Points 1-4. Transfer these measurements from where the Center Line meets t
entries.searchText:
Round to Square Information required:
True height
Square dimensions
Round dimensions
Step 1. Draw an Elevation View of the fitting (include insulation).
Step 2. Draw a Plan View. Trisect the top right quarter circle and label as shown.
Step 3. From the Plan View, take the measurements from Point A to Points 1-4.
Transfer these measurements from where the Center Line meets the
horizontal line to the right side on the Elevation View. Label these points.
Step 4. Draw lines from the Apex to these points. These lines are the Slant Heights.
Step 5. For the Stretch-out, draw a line the exact length of a square side and label
the ends A. From the Elevation View set dividers at the Apex & Points 1-4
on the Elevation View. Transfer this length to the Stretch-out by using
both A Points as an Apex. Swing arcs, and where they intersect is Point 1.
Step 6. Set dividers from 1 to 2 on the Plan View and swing arcs to each side of
Point 1 on the Stretch-out.
Step 7. From the Elevation View, set dividers at the Apex and Points 2-3 on the
Elevation View and transfer this measure to the Stretch-out again using
both A Points as an Apex. Where these arcs meet, is Point 2.
Step 8. Use the same methods from Steps 6 and 7 to form Points 3 and 4.
Step 9. Draw an arc to each side of Point A using 2 the measure of a square side.
Step 10. From the Elevation View, set the dividers at the Apex and Point $4 and
transfer to the Stretch-out using Points 4 as the Apex points. Where they
intersect with the last arcs, are the $ Points.
Step 11. Connect all points to form half of the fitting. Add for lap, crimp and bead,

entries.title: Local 1 Handbook: 37. Square to Square Transition
entries.summary: 37 | Square to Square Transition | Information required: Include insulation in measurements | a. Small square dimensions, b. Large square dimensions, ¢. True height Step 1. Draw both Elevation and Plan Views. On the Plan View draw lines to the various points as shown in Figure 2, and label those points as shown. Also on the Elevation View draw a Center Line
entries.searchText:
Square to Square Transition 37 |
Square to Square Transition |
Information required: Include insulation in measurements |
a. Small square dimensions, b. Large square dimensions, ¢. True height
Step 1. Draw both Elevation and Plan Views. On the Plan View draw lines to the
various points as shown in Figure 2, and label those points as shown. Also
on the Elevation View draw a Center Line dividing the fitting in half.
Step 2. From the Plan View take measurements from B-1 and transfer it to the
Elevation View. This measurement is placed on the horizontal line going
from the Center Line to the right. Do the same for Points A-2 and A-3. As
®@ this is an equal sized fitting, we only need to measure one quarter of the
fitting. Label these three points as shown.
e Step 3. To start the layout, draw a line the width of the large square. Divide this
line into 2 equal parts and label as shown in Figure 3. From Point B draw a
@ 90 degree vertical line; this will be the Center Line.
@ Step 4. From the Elevation View, set dividers at Slant Height B-1 and transfer this
set to the layout going from point B to an intersecting point on the Center
e Line to form Point 1.
Step 5. Draw a 90 degree horizontal line to each side of Point 1. These lines to
r ) each side will be half the dimension of a small square side. The ends of
these lines will be labeled Point 2.
@ Step 6. From both Points 2, swing arcs to either side using half the small square
dimensions.
© Step 7. From the elevation view, set dividers at Slant Height A-3 and transfer to
the layout by using A as the Apex and going to an intersecting point on
@ the last arcs drawn. Label this point as Point 3.
Step 8. Set dividers at slant height B-1 and swing arcs off to the side of Point A,
@ using Point 3 as an Apex. Then using half the large square dimension, set
@ dividers at Point A and swing an intersecting arc to the last arc drawn to
obtain Point C.
@ Step 9. Connect all points to form half the fitting. For the other half, add a lap.

entries.searchText:
Oval to Round a I a a a |
___Oval to Round 38
ca) _ Information required: 1
4 a, Oval dimensions with insulation included, b. Round dimensions ;
eo c. True height |
| Step 1. Draw a plan view of the fitting.
© Step 2. Trisect and label the small circle as shown. |
® _ Step 3. Trisect and label the bottom ‘4 circles of the oval.
Step 4. Draw a 90 degree angle and take the measurements for the slant heights.
© 4 These measurements are taken from 5 to 1, 1 to 6, 6 to 2, 2 to 7, 7 to 3, 3to
4 8 and 8 to 4. Place all these measurements on the horizontal line. Measure
@ ‘ from the Apex to points on the horizontal line and label as shown.
Step 5. Start the stretch-out by drawing a horizontal line equal to the distance
© from 8 to 8 on the Plan View.
_ Step 6. From the slant heights set dividers from the Apex to the 8-4 Point and i
@ transfer to the stretch-out by using Point 8 as an Apex and swing arcs that
will intersect to acquire Point 4.
© Step 7. From the Plan View, set dividers from 1 to 2 and on the stretch-out set the
@ dividers on Point 4 and swing an arc to each side. |
Step 8. Return to the slant heights and set the dividers from the Apex to the 8-3
e@ Point and on the stretch-out, using Point 8 as an Apex swing intersecting
arcs to form Points 3 on the stretch-out.
® Step 9. From the plan view, set dividers from 7 to 8 and swing arcs to the outside
of both Points 8.
C2) Step 10. From the slant heights, set your dividers at the 7-3 Point and transfer this
measurement to the stretch-out using Point 3 as an Apex. This arc will
@ intersect to form Point 7.
Step 11. Continue this formula to obtain all the points for the layout.
@ Step 12. Connect points, laps and notches to obtain half the layout.

entries.summary: ® 48 @ Information required: @ Heel and Throat measurements Step 1. Obtain Heel and Throat measurements using the Machinist Figure Method @ or from a Miter Chart. Add 3/4" to the Heel measurement and 3/8" to the Throat measurement. @ Step 2. Draw a 90 degree angle with the vertical line to the left side. Step 3. Take the Heel measurement plus 3/4" and divide
entries.searchText:
Formula for a Gore Pattern (Regular Method) ® 48
@ Information required:
@ Heel and Throat measurements
Step 1. Obtain Heel and Throat measurements using the Machinist Figure Method
@ or from a Miter Chart. Add 3/4" to the Heel measurement and 3/8" to the
Throat measurement.
@ Step 2. Draw a 90 degree angle with the vertical line to the left side.
Step 3. Take the Heel measurement plus 3/4" and divide by 2 and mark this
@ number on the vertical line from the base line. Take the Throat
measurement plus 3/8" and divide by 2 and mark this number on
@ the vertical line.
@ Step 4. Find the exact center of both points and draw a semi-circle from this
point as shown in Figure 1. Trisect this semi-circle into six equal parts and
re) label as shown.
Step 5. Draw 90 degree lines up from the Base Line to the points on the ‘4 circle.
@ Step 6. Divide the circumference by twice the number of equally divided parts
of the semi-circle and draw the Stretch-out for the gore pattern as in
@ Figure 2.
Step 7. Number all the lines of the stretch-out as shown.
Ca) Step 8. Take measurements from the base line to numbered points on the
circle and transfer to corresponding numbered lines on the Stretch-out.
Go Step 9. Connect all the points on the stretch-out to form the gore pattern.
@ Step10. Add for a lap on one end or the other. Use your center punch to mark
where the bead and crimp will start and terminate (usually the 4 points).

onap-Line Gores

@ Length of a gore
@ Step 1. To determine a gore's width, place a straight edge horizontally to
the curvature of the tank and measure the distance that the
@ straight edge rests against the tank. Allow for 1/8" on each side.
Step 2. Divide the width of the gore into the total circumference to

@ equal number gores to equal the total circumference.

8 the tank top starts to curve. Snap the chalk line there.
Step 5. Divide the width of a gore by two and, using this measurement,
tT ) mark points to either side of the heel of the gore.

Step 7. Divide the distance from the tank top to the heel of the gore into
e@ equal parts (six or eight). Number these divisions accordingly.
Step 8. Measure the widths and transfer to the corresponding
@ numbered line on the Stretch-out.
e Step 9. Add 3/8" to each side at each division for the bead and crimp.

Step 1. Draw a horizontal line one half the diameter of the tank.
@ Step 2. Draw a vertical line equal to the height of the tank with insulation included. Carry this
2 line down from the tank top to a point that equals the full diameter of the tank. This
bottom point is the Apex.
r ) Step 3. Set your dividers from the Apex to the tank top and swing an arc to form the tank top.
Step 4. Divide the diameter of the tank top by ten. Use this measurement to swing an
@ intersecting arc to form the curve where the tank top and the side of the vessel meet.
Step 5. Divide the tank top into six or eight equal parts and label.
@ Step 6. Place a straight edge on the tank top head and allowing a gap of 1/8" on each side.
Obtain a Heel measurement. Round up or down as necessary to match the equal
[ ) number of gores required to surround the total circumference.

@ the right, and then draw a line from that point to the Apex as shown.

@ crimp line. Label those lines.

@ and doing a six division walk-out as shown. Label those lines as shown.
Step 11. Transfer the measurements from the bead and crimp line to corresponding numbered
r ) lines on the Stretch-out.
Step 12. Connect all the points on the Stretch-out to form a pattern for the gores for the tank top.

Step 4. Divide the result of Step 3 by the result of Step 2: 2000 -:- 25= 80.

Formula: b. = The Square Root of a Squared + b Squared.

C = Square root of 80 squared + 25 squared
® C = square root of 6400 + 625.

) C = Square root of 40 squared + 12.5 squared
C = Square root of 1600 + 156.25
a) C = Square root of 1756.25 © = 41.9076 or 42. (rounded off)
Slant height 3: Slant height 1- Slant height 2. 84 - 42 = 42
Co) IV. Objective: To Calculate the Number of Degrees in Each Cone
Formula: 180 degrees x large diameter divided by Slant Height #1
@ 180 x 50 -:- by 84 = 107.1428 degrees
V. Objective: Find the Number of Pieces required for the cone.
@ The circumference of the large diameter is 157.079,
@ so using the trial and error process we determine that:
6 pieces will be sufficient for this application
@ Vi. Objective: Determine the Arc Length of each Piece
157.079 -:- 6 = 26.1798, 26.1798 will be the arc length of each piece.
@ Vil. Objective: Find the amount of Degrees in the Central Angle
To find the amount of degrees in the central angle, divide the degrees
@ in each cone by the number of pieces required for the cone
107.1428 -:- 6 = 17.857 degrees or rounded off will be 18 degrees:
| @ Thus half the central angle will be 9 degrees.

entries.searchText:
Determining the Radius of a Curve (Geometric & Algebraic) Eee
- Determining the Radius of a Curve
C3) Geometric Method:
e Step 1. Place a piece of cardboard behind the pipe and draw a section of the
curve.
Step 2. Mark any three points along the arc and label them A, B & C. Draw lines
© from A to B and from B to C.
@ Step 3. Bisect the A and B line by swinging arcs from both Points A and B. Do the
same for the B & C line.
@ Step 4. Extend lines down through the inter-sectors to form an Apex. From this
Apex point to the arc of the pipe will be the radius of the curve.
* Figure |
e@ — a2
@ FN \L/
iJ
| @ Chord
[ ) CLR: A line between two
Center points on an arc
@ Line Radius
e Apex
Algebraic Method:
i) Step 1. Draw Step 1 from the Geometric Method.
@ Step 2. Mark two points on the arc and label them A & B.
Step 3. Draw a line from A to B. This will be the length or L.
@ Step 4. Find the center of L and draw a 90 degree line up
to the arc. This line will be H for the height.
@ The formula to find the radius is:
R = 4 H squared + L squared
@ 8H
Example: L = 24 and H=2
@ R= (4x2x2)+ (24x24) R= 164576 592
@ 8x2 16 = 16
Radius = 37"

Industrial Insulation
PHASE 2

Produced by

Michael Kelly
© SOLAS 2014

Module 2– Unit 3
Industrial Insulation Phase 2
1
Parallel Line Development
Revision 2.0, August 2014
Unit Objective
By the end of this unit each apprentice will be able to:
 Demonstrate the use of parallel line development.
 Determine joint allowances.
 Develop patterns with joint allowances.
 Develop the pattern for a two piece elbow.
