entries.title: Insulators Field Reference Section1
entries.summary: Insulator’s Field Reference Manual Author: Snowman Section 1: Introduction & Safety Essentials This field reference combines verified technical data, layout charts, and applied geometry methods for professional heat- and frost-insulation workers. It condenses the Heat & Frost Insulators Handbook, Industrial Insulation Training Modules, and related guides int

paragraphs.text: This field reference combines verified technical data, layout charts, and applied geometry methods for professional heat- and frost-insulation workers. It condenses the Heat & Frost Insulators Handbook, Industrial Insulation Training Modules, and related guides into a portable, job-ready format.

The manual is designed for: - Field insulators and apprentices referencing on-site patterns. - Foremen verifying firestopping and jacketing compliance. - Trades educators delivering layout geometry or fitting-development training.
Head: Hard hat meeting CSA Z94.1.
Eyes: Safety glasses or goggles — mandatory when cutting metal or using power snips.
Respiratory: NIOSH approved P95 mask when handling fibrous or dusty insulation.
Body: Long sleeves and cuffs fastened.
Confirm confined-space and hot-work permits before removal or installation.
Use caution with power tools; all edges of jacketing metal are razor-sharp.
“Measure twice, cut once.” — Minimize material waste and sharp-edge rework.
Maintain clean drop zones and secure scrap to avoid fall hazards.
Follow IBC 714 Penetration and Firestop System requirements.
Verify that through-penetrations are sealed with an ASTM E814 or UL 1479-tested assembly before closing walls.
Confirm each installed system’s rating matches the rated assembly.
When uncertain, reference manufacturer’s listings or approved Engineering Judgments (EJs).
Respect live conduit and energized circuits — consult Electrical Conduit Chart for size and load limits.
Allow adequate clearances between insulation, wiring, and heat tracing systems.
Avoid overreaching when measuring overhead ducts; use telescopic tools.
For cold-weather work: apply wind-chill factor and rest break table from K-factor section later in this book.

entries.searchText: Insulators Field Reference Section1 Reference Charts AI/Insulators_Field_Reference_Section1.docx Insulator’s Field Reference Manual Author: Snowman Section 1: Introduction & Safety Essentials This field reference combines verified technical data, layout charts, and applied geometry methods for professional heat- and frost-insulation workers. It condenses the Heat & Frost Insulators Handbook, Industrial Insulation Training Modules, and related guides into a portable, job-ready format. 1.2 Intended Use The manual is designed for: - Field insulators and apprentices referencing on-site patterns. - Foremen verifying firestopping and jacketing compliance. - Trades educators delivering layout geometry or fitting-development training. 1.3 Safety Essentials Personal Protective Equipment (PPE): Head: Hard hat meeting CSA Z94.1. Eyes: Safety glasses or goggles — mandatory when cutting metal or using power snips. Hands: Cut-resistant gloves (steel-mesh or Kevlar). Respiratory: NIOSH approved P95 mask when handling fibrous or dusty insulation. Body: Long sleeves and cuffs fastened. Worksite Practices: Confirm confined-space and hot-work permits before removal or installation. Use caution with power tools; all edges of jacketing metal are razor-sharp. “Measure twice, cut once.” — Minimize material waste and sharp-edge rework. Maintain clean drop zones and secure scrap to avoid fall hazards. Firestop and Penetration Safety: Follow IBC 714 Penetration and Firestop System requirements. Verify that through-penetrations are sealed with an ASTM E814 or UL 1479-tested assembly before closing walls. Confirm each installed system’s rating matches the rated assembly. When uncertain, reference manufacturer’s listings or approved Engineering Judgments (EJs). Electrical and Mechanical Awareness: Respect live conduit and energized circuits — consult Electrical Conduit Chart for size and load limits. Allow adequate clearances between insulation, wiring, and heat tracing systems. Environmental and Ergonomic Tips: Avoid overreaching when measuring overhead ducts; use telescopic tools. For cold-weather work: apply wind-chill factor and rest break table from K-factor section later in this book. 1.4 Safety Checklist (Quick Reference) 1.5 QR Reference for Section 1 Task | Check Box PPE on and inspected | ☐ Firestop details verified | ☐ Hot-work permit issued | ☐ Fall-protection anchorage confirmed | ☐ Electrical clearances checked | ☐ Area clean and tagged safe | ☐ Topic | Suggested Public Video Source PPE donning and removal demo | CSA Group Safety Training YouTube Firestop inspection overview | International Firestop Council (YouTube) Safe metal cutting and crimping | HVAC Sheet Metal Basics Channel Confined space safety intro | WorkSafe BC Official Channel

entries.title: Insulators Field Reference Section10
entries.summary: Section 10: Final Commissioning & Turnover Procedures 10.1 Overview This section defines the procedures for final commissioning and turnover of insulation systems. It ensures that all systems meet design intent, safety standards, and client acceptance criteria.

paragraphs.text: This section defines the procedures for final commissioning and turnover of insulation systems. It ensures that all systems meet design intent, safety standards, and client acceptance criteria. Applicable references include ASTM C1710, ISO 9001, and CSA Z94 for QA/QC verification.
Conduct thermal performance retest (ΔT ≤ 3 °C variance from baseline).
Verify pressure and leak test results for insulated systems.
Confirm adhesive and sealant cure per manufacturer specifications.
Document results using standardized inspection forms.
All punch-list items are tracked using unique identifiers (e.g., PL-001, PL-002). Each deficiency must include a description, responsible party, and resolution status.
Conduct joint walk-through with client, inspector, and project manager.
Review all punch-list items for completion prior to sign-off.
Record notes, photos, and comments in meeting minutes.
Obtain final acceptance signature from client representative.
Assemble QA/QC turnover package with indexed binder and digital copy.
Include inspection reports, test results, and photo documentation.
Ensure all sign-off sheets are dated and initialed by responsible parties.
Submit digital copies to client portal and archive for minimum five years.
Provide client training session covering system maintenance requirements.
Distribute as-built drawings and product data sheets.
Record attendance and collect signatures from participants.
File training records within project QA turnover package.
Schedule 30-day post-turnover inspection for thermal stability verification.
Compare temperature performance to baseline data (variance ≤ 5%).
Address deviations through corrective actions and record in QA log.
Archive updated as-built documentation and submit summary report.

entries.title: Insulators Field Reference Section3
entries.summary: Section 3: Basic Geometry & Layout Methods 3.1 Introduction to Layout Geometry Layout or pattern development refers to unfolding three-dimensional shapes into flat patterns for fabrication and fitting. Industrial insulation layouts typically use three primary methods: Parallel-line development, Radial-line development, and Triangulation.

paragraphs.text: Layout or pattern development refers to unfolding three-dimensional shapes into flat patterns for fabrication and fitting. Industrial insulation layouts typically use three primary methods: Parallel-line development, Radial-line development, and Triangulation. Each method converts the geometry of a fitting or component into a flat, cut-ready pattern.

The parallel-line method applies to cylinders, elbows, and rectangular prisms. Lines are drawn parallel to each other, allowing direct transfer of dimensions from elevation views. Typical applications include two-piece elbows, straight tees, and pipe segments.
Place the seam on the shortest side for efficiency.
Account for joint types such as groove, slip, or peined joints.
Avoid cumulative error when dividing arcs for layout.
Figure 3-1: Truncated Cylinder Layout (Parallel-Line Development) [Colour retained]
Figure 3-2: Two-Piece Elbow Pattern and Elevation [Colour retained]

Radial-line development is used for conical or tapered shapes such as reducers and transitions. It relies on measuring true lengths from a common apex to create accurate flat layouts. Each generator line of the cone becomes a radial element in the pattern.
Draw the cone elevation and determine the true length of its side.
Swing arcs from the apex to mark true lengths on the pattern base.
Divide the base circle into equal parts and transfer points to the pattern.

The triangulation method applies when both ends of a fitting differ in shape or size, such as eccentric reducers or offset transitions. The pattern is formed by creating a network of true-length triangles, each corresponding to surfaces on the fitting.
Draw both end profiles of the fitting and connect corresponding points with projection lines.
Find true lengths by constructing triangles perpendicular to the projection plane.
Transfer the true lengths to the pattern layout, connecting sequentially to form the complete shape.
Figure 3-4: Eccentric Reducer – Triangulation Example [Colour retained]
Align baselines along sheet edges to minimize waste.
Label construction lines clearly (A–A, B–B, etc.) for reference.
Lay out simpler fittings first to optimize space and workflow.

Industrial insulation layouts typically use three primary methods: Parallel-line development, Radial-line development, and Triangulation. Each method converts the geometry of a fitting or component into a flat, cut-ready pattern. 3.2 Parallel-Line Development The parallel-line method applies to cylinders, elbows, and rectangular prisms. Lines are drawn parallel to each other, allowing direct transfer of dimensions from elevation views. Typical applications include two-piece elbows, straight tees, and pipe segments. Field Notes: Place the seam on the shortest side for efficiency. Account for joint types such as groove, slip, or peined joints. Avoid cumulative error when dividing arcs for layout. Figure 3-1: Truncated Cylinder Layout (Parallel-Line Development) [Colour retained] Figure 3-2: Two-Piece Elbow Pattern and Elevation [Colour retained] 3.3 Radial-Line Development Radial-line development is used for conical or tapered shapes such as reducers and transitions. It relies on measuring true lengths from a common apex to create accurate flat layouts. Each generator line of the cone becomes a radial element in the pattern. Steps: Draw the cone elevation and determine the true length of its side. Swing arcs from the apex to mark true lengths on the pattern base. Divide the base circle into equal parts and transfer points to the pattern. Figure 3-3: Radial-Line Cone Layout [Colour retained] 3.4 Triangulation Method The triangulation method applies when both ends of a fitting differ in shape or size, such as eccentric reducers or offset transitions. The pattern is formed by creating a network of true-length triangles, each corresponding to surfaces on the fitting. Procedure: Draw both end profiles of the fitting and connect corresponding points with projection lines. Find true lengths by constructing triangles perpendicular to the projection plane. Transfer the true lengths to the pattern layout, connecting sequentially to form the complete shape. Figure 3-4: Eccentric Reducer – Triangulation Example [Colour retained] 3.5 Shape Math Quick Reference 3.6 Efficient Drawing & Field Sequencing Align baselines along sheet edges to minimize waste. Label construction lines clearly (A–A, B–B, etc.) for reference. entries.title: Insulators Field Reference Section4
entries.summary: Section 4: Pipe & Duct Layout Patterns 4.1 Overview Accurate pattern development minimizes waste and ensures consistent insulation coverage. The same geometric logic used for metal jacketing applies to removable insulation covers.

paragraphs.text: Accurate pattern development minimizes waste and ensures consistent insulation coverage. The same geometric logic used for metal jacketing applies to removable insulation covers. This section provides layout methods and field-ready dimensional tables for elbows, reducers, tees, wyes, and offsets.

Elbows are the most common fittings in insulation layout. Layouts may be two-piece, three-piece, five-piece, or seven-piece depending on desired accuracy and radius. Each pattern begins with an elevation view and step-off divisions based on the number of gores.
Field Formula: Arc Length = π × Diameter × (Angle ÷ 360)
Use fewer segments for small diameters or tight-radius bends.
Increase the number of gores for large diameters to improve smoothness.
Ensure equal divisions between inner and outer arcs.

Reducers connect different pipe diameters and can be concentric or eccentric. Radial-line or triangulation methods are used depending on the taper angle. Eccentric reducers maintain one side flat for proper drainage or clearance.
Figure 4-2: Concentric and Eccentric Reducer Layouts [Colour retained]

Tees and wyes are complex intersections requiring precise projection between the run and branch. Equal tees divide the main run at 90°, while wyes typically intersect at 45°. Layouts use parallel or radial projection from the intersection line.
Figure 4-3: Tee and Wye Intersection Layouts [Colour retained]

Offsets are used to connect misaligned ducts or pipes. Geometry relies on rise/run ratios and accurate transfer of centerline dimensions. Goose necks consist of multiple offsets for smoother transitions.
Figure 4-4: Duct Offset and Goose Neck Layout [Colour retained]
Label each piece with diameter, radius, and joint type before cutting.
Test-fit with paper or foil before transferring to metal.
Number gores sequentially to ensure correct assembly.
Use magnetic or digital protractors to confirm angles.

entries.title: Insulators Field Reference Section5
entries.summary: Section 5: Material Specifications & Installation Standards 5.1 Overview This section outlines materials, performance standards, and installation practices for insulation systems. All materials and assemblies shall comply with CSA, ASTM, and UL standards for safety, efficiency, and durability.

paragraphs.text: This section outlines materials, performance standards, and installation practices for insulation systems. All materials and assemblies shall comply with CSA, ASTM, and UL standards for safety, efficiency, and durability.

Selecting the right insulation material ensures performance under design temperature and service conditions. Common materials include fiberglass, mineral wool, foam glass, elastomeric rubber, and urethane foam.
Insulation thickness varies with pipe size and service temperature. The following are general recommendations.
Vapor barriers prevent condensation and corrosion. Weather jacketing protects insulation from mechanical damage and UV exposure.
Stainless Steel: for corrosive or high-impact environments.
PVC: economical indoor finish for cold systems.
ASJ (All-Service Jacket): foil-scrim-kraft vapor barrier for indoor use.
Firestopping ensures penetrations maintain rated fire resistance. Only listed and tested systems may be used.
ASTM E814 / UL 1479 – Through-penetration firestop systems.
ASTM E1966 / UL 2079 – Fire-resistance of joint systems.
ASTM E2174 – On-site inspection of installed firestops.
Verify system identification and UL listing.
Ensure correct annular space and sealant materials.
Figure 5-3: UL Firestop System Example [Colour retained]
Cut insulation cleanly and square using sharp tools.
Apply adhesives evenly; avoid gaps and drips.
Maintain tolerances: ±3 mm circumference, ±6 mm length.
Record installation data (tag number, material, location).
Visual Inspection – confirm continuous coverage and sealed joints.
Adhesion Testing – perform periodic pull tests.
Thermal Testing – reference ASTM C680 for heat-loss calculations.
Label Verification – ensure each penetration has correct UL ID.

entries.title: Insulators Field Reference Section9
entries.summary: Section 9: Project Communication & Reporting Procedures 9.1 Overview This section outlines the communication and reporting procedures required to maintain coordination between field, supervision, and management. Procedures align with CSA Z1005 and ISO 9001 standards to ensure consistent documentation and quality assurance.

paragraphs.text: This section outlines the communication and reporting procedures required to maintain coordination between field, supervision, and management. Procedures align with CSA Z1005 and ISO 9001 standards to ensure consistent documentation and quality assurance.
Attendance and crew hours logged correctly.
Completed work scope recorded with drawing references.
Material usage, deliveries, and waste tracked.
Safety observations summarized with corrective actions.
Attach site photos for documentation of key work areas.
Submit RFIs with a unique ID number (RFI-001, RFI-002, etc.).
Include clear description, photo, and drawing reference for each RFI.
Submit proposed change (PCO) with estimated cost and schedule impact.
Obtain approval through the digital sign-off process prior to work change.
Progress reporting is based on the percent-complete method by system or area. Weekly reporting shall include earned value metrics (EV, PV, AC) to track project performance.
Prepare standard agenda templates for all coordination meetings.
Record attendees, key discussion points, and assigned action items.
Use an action matrix with colour-coded status (Open, Closed, Pending).
Store signed minutes within 24 hours in shared project drive.
Retain all records for at least one year after project completion.
Figure 9-4: Meeting Action Matrix Example [Colour retained]
Ensure all controlled copies are distributed via approved systems.
Maintain version history and mark obsolete files clearly.
Confirm all field copies are updated to latest revisions.
Escalation follows a clear communication hierarchy to resolve issues promptly. All escalation events must be documented in the daily report or meeting minutes.

1. Transfer the gore settings from the gore ruler as shown in fig. 1
2. Use the square to draw 90-degree lines through each mark, lines should be at least 1" longer than the 1/2 the heel measurement. See figure 2.
3. Line 1 is the throat line. Add 3/8" for the beat to the throat measurement. See figure 3.

4. Line #9 is your heel line. Add 3/4" for the crimp to your heel measurement and then divide by 2. Transfer the length to line #9. Example 6" Heel + 3/4" crimp = 6-3/4". Divide this by 2 to get 3-3/8". Transfer this to line 9.

5. Find your circumference (3.142 X Diameter) and draw a line this length. Divide this line into 16 equal parts. Now we transfer the lengths from step 4 to our circumference line as shown below. Now the gore pattern is complete. See figure 4

1. Measure outer diameter of pipe, divided by 2 to get radius. 2. Locate the radius on the T-Master (between A&B). 3. While holding the T-Master steady in one position, trace the curve at this radius and transfer the 4. squaring tabs as shown on the reverse. 5. Remove the T-Master and connect the squaring tab marks, this will give you your 90 lines.

31International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
Firestopping GuidelinesNotes:
Inspector Pocket Guide
The following information is intended to
provide construction and code enforcement
professionals with basic checkpoints to ensure
that the required fire resistance ratings are
maintained when through penetrations and
linear joints breach walls and floors.
This inspection guideline is not intended to
be all encompassing or to be used as a design
guide. It is for information and educational
purposes only.
The Source of Firestop Expertise
SM

4 International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
Scope
Construction codes have very clear require-
ments on passive fire protection. These require-
ments are included in Chapter 7 Fire and Smoke
Protection Features of the International Code
Council (ICC) International Building Code
© (IBC©).
Whenever required by the IBC©, the fire
resistance ratings of floors, walls, floor/ceiling,
roof/ceiling assemblies or fire-resistance-rated
duct enclosures must be restored when an open-
ing is made to accommodate penetrations for
mechanical, electrical, plumbing, communication
systems and ventilation ducts. joints between
floors, walls, floors and walls, etc, must also have
the same fire resistance ratings as the adjacent
construction.
NFPA 101 (Life Safety Code), NFPA 70 (National
Electric Code), the International Mechanical
Code
© (IMC©) and the International Plumbing
Code© (IPC©) also include provisions related to
the protection of penetrations. The codes have
explicit requirements for inspection of firestop
systems before they are concealed.
The IBC 2012 107.2.2 requires that evidence be
submitted to the building official showing that
the materials and methods of construction used
to protect penetrations, joints and ventilation
ducts in fire resistance rated building elements
shall not reduce the required fire resistance
rating. The International Fire Code
© has require-
ments for periodic inspection of firestop systems
throughout the life of the building.
Building Code Requirements

Lorem Ipsum
5International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
Scope
The authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) must
review and approve firestop system details and
rated ducted enclosures. Hence, firestop systems
details and materials must be included on the
plans and specifications. Manufacturer’s cut
sheets are often accepted if they are generated by
an approved testing agency. If details, products
and specifications are not sufficient to provide
clear directions to the general contractor and
firestop installer, the submittals should be noted
as incomplete and returned to the designer to be
resubmitted with the required information. If the
plans and specifications are clear and complete,
most field problems with firestop systems can be
avoided.
Plans Examination / Review
Lorem Ipsum
28 International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
Firestopping GuidelinesAbout the International Code Council
The International Code Council is a member-fo-
cused association dedicated to helping the build-
ing safety community and construction industry
provide safe, sustainable and affordable construc-
tion through the development of codes and stan-
dards used in the design, build and compliance
process. Most U.S. communities and many global
markets choose the International Codes. ICC
Evaluation Service (ICC-ES), a subsidiary of the
International Code Council, has been the industry
leader in performing technical evaluations for
code compliance fostering safe and sustainable
design and construction.
Headquarters:
500 New Jersey Avenue, NW, 6th Floor,
Washington, DC 20001-2070
District Offices:
Birmingham, AL; Chicago. IL; Los Angeles, CA
1-888-422-7233
www.iccsafe.org

Lorem Ipsum
6 International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
ScopeEngineering Judgments
It is not unusual to find, in construction
projects, unique conditions which have not
been tested and listed, that require special
consideration. The protection of these conditions
will necessitate Engineering Judgments (EJ’s)
since they have not been tested and do not
comply with a published design listing.
The International Firestop Council has
published “Recommended IFC Guidelines for
Evaluating Firestop Systems in Engineering
Judgments” to assist designers, plan reviewers
and inspectors in addressing nonconforming
construction details. The plan submittals should
always indicate which details are based on EJ’s.
The submitted EJ’s need to be approved by the
building official, and made available to the field
inspector when approved.
The IFC guidelines for the evaluation of EJ’s can be
obtained from the IFC website: www.firestop.org

Lorem Ipsum
7International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
Scope
The time allocated for inspections can be
drastically reduced if the proper paperwork is
provided on the approved plans. Planning and
communication between the building designer,
structural engineer and the installer prior to
construction will save time, costs and resources
in assuring the application of the proper systems.
Verification of system testing and listings
with a nationally recognized laboratory, prior
to installation in the field, is key to a smooth
inspection process. Use of applicable ASTM
practices will provide guidelines for inspection
of installed systems.
Inspection Process

8 International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
The ability of penetration firestop systems,
fire resistive joint systems and ventilation
systems to perform their intended function of
fire containment is directly related to the quality
of their installations. Thorough inspection is an
integral component of any passive fire protection
quality control program. It is not realistic to
visually inspect each penetration and the entire
length of every joint and ventilation duct. How
many inspections are enough? This is a judgment
call by the inspector; however, the ASTM
inspection standards may be used as a guideline.
Field Inspection Process
Scope
Major elements of quality firestop
inspections are:
• Firestop systems must not be concealed from
view before being inspected and approved
(IBC 110.3.6).
• Walk through visual inspections should be
made during the rough and final inspections.
• When necessary or required, destructive
evaluation will be made on various types of
firestop systems.
• Appropriate tools for firestop inspections
should include a flashlight, measuring device
and cutting tool.
• Proper material depths, annular space, at-
tachments, spacing and product type are
critical to the effectiveness of the system.

Lorem Ipsum
9International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
Scope
• Construction documents detailing the fire-
stop locations and systems must be kept on
site to assist in the conduct of the inspection.
• Insure to a reasonable degree that empty
containers, wrappings or boxes of the speci-
fied materials are in sufficient quantity to
have been installed correctly.
• Insure to a reasonable degree that the actual
products, containers, wrappings or boxes
are labeled with the approved testing agency
marks and are as specified in the submitted
details.
• Measure the depth and width of materials as
indicated in the details (sometimes density
measurements are also required for products
such as thermal insulation).
• Insure to a reasonable degree that joints
have been installed in such manner that the
required movement can be achieved.
• Compare the installed firestop system with
the approved submitted details.
• Insure a reasonable degree of workmanship,
which would indicate compliance with the
specified design.
Lorem Ipsum

There are several independent testing
laboratories, also referred to as third party
testing agencies, which conduct the fire testing
of firestop, perimeter fire barrier and duct
enclosure systems. The fire test results are
usually included as design listings in the fire
resistance directories published by the testing
laboratory. These Directories are an important
source of information during the plan review
process and inspection process. The following are
some of the recognized independent laboratories
conducting tests of firestop systems:
1.
Underwriters Laboratories Inc.
Northbrook, IL
(847) 272-8800
www.ul.com
2.
Southwest Research Institute
San Antonio, TX
(210) 522-2311
www.fire.swri.org
3.
Factory Mutual
Norwood, MA
(781) 762-4300
www.fmglobal.com
4.
Intertek Testing Services
San Antonio, TX
(210) 625-8100
www.intertek.com
Directories
Third Party Testing Agencies:
Independent
Testing
Laboratories
12
Step One:
Firestopping Inspection Manual
International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Guidelines
Step Two:
Verify the rating of the joint system is equal to the
rating of the assemblies it is connecting. The code
requires that the rating of a joint system shall not
be less than the fire resistive ratings of the adjacent
assemblies.
Step Three:
Verify the documents and submitted drawings
reference systems that have been tested for the
required amount of movement. A system listing a
nominal 1 inch joint width with 25% compression or
extension, actually allows for a movement of 1/4” of
compression and 1/4” of extension.
Verify the documents and submitted drawings have
been reviewed by the Project Design Professional
and/or the structural engineer and that they meet the
allowable movement requirements.
Verify the documents and submitted drawings
reference tested and listed fire resistive joint systems
tested to ASTM E1966 or UL 2079 by accredited testing
laboratories or certified third party testing agencies.
These systems should be published or readily available
via the internet or other means.
For Fire Resistive Joint Systems:
Including Mechanical
Fire Barrier Systems
Step Four:

13
Firestopping Inspection Manual
International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
A. Is the joint system tested and listed?
B. Is the joint system tested for the amount of
movement required?
C. Is the joint system tested for the class and type
of movement required?
D. Is the fire rating of the joint equal to (or
greater than) the assemblies it is adjacent to?
E. Observe the nominal installed width of the
joint at the framing inspection.
F. If a mechanical system is used, are the
specified tracks installed with a third party
testing agency label attached?
G. Do the supplied products have labels from
recognized quality assurance agency?
H. Does the field installation follow the listing?
a. Specified forming, packing or
backing material?
b. Specified type of sealant, coating or device?
c. Specified amount, depth, location of
sealant, coating or device?
d. Specified accessory items – cover plates,
bond breaker tape, and specified deflection
track?

Guidelines
As an overview of these steps, verify the
parameters indicated in the system are the same
as those installed in the field: (Download checklist
form at the IFC web site www.firestop.org).

Firestopping Inspection Manual
International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Test Standards relevant to Firestop Systems:
1. ASTM E 814 (ANSI/UL 1479) “Standard Test Method
for Fire Tests of Through-Penetration Fire Stops”
2. ASTM E 1966 (ANSI/UL 2079) “Standards Test
Method for Fire-Resistive Joint Systems”
3. ASTM E 1399 “Cyclic Movement and Measuring
the Minimum and Maximum Joint Widths of
Architectural Joint Systems”
4. ASTM E 2174 “Standard Practice for On-Site
Inspection of Installed Fire Stops”
5. ASTM E 2307 “Standard Test Method for
Determining the Fire Resistance of Perimeter Fire
Barrier Systems Using the Intermediate Scale,
Multi-Story Test Apparatus”
6. ASTM E 2393 “Standard Practice for On-Site
Inspection of Installed Fire Resistive Joint System
and Perimeter Fire Barriers”
7. ASTM E 2336 “Standard Test Methods for Fire
Resistive Grease Duct Enclosure Systems”
8. ISO 6944 “Fire Resistance Tests – Ventilation Ducts”
9. ICC ES AC179 “ Acceptance Criteria for Metallic
HV AC Duct Enclosure Assemblies”
10. ASTM E-2750 “Standard Guide for Extension
of Data from Firestop Penetration System Tests
Conducted in Accordance with ASTM E814”
Plan Review And Inspection
Guidelines For
Firestop Systems
Applicable Standards:
Guidelines

19
Firestopping Inspection Manual
International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Guidelines
Verify the field installation is consistent with the
parameters of the listing and therefore compliant.
A. Duct System Type – kitchen grease exhaust,
hazardous material exhaust, ventilation,
supply/ return, etc.
B. Duct Construction – dimensions, material,
gauge, reinforcement, connections, vertical or
horizontal orientation.
C. Enclosure System – labeled components,
number of layers, fire rating, required
clearance to combustibles, thickness and
density of material, material joints (overlap of
material, taping of cut edges or seams), etc.
D. Enclosure System Attachment – mechanical
method of attachment to duct (typically steel
banding and/or capacitor discharge insulation
pins), components, spacing, gauge, etc.
E. Duct Supports – hanger system components,
frequency of location, clearance to enclosure
system, protection requirements.
F. Access Door – field fabricated or prefabricated
door construction and protection with
enclosure system material must match design
listing.
G. Firestop System – refer to design listing for fire
rated assembly construction, annular space,
packing material type and depth, and firestop
material type and depth.
Step Four:
construction assembly penetrated. The F and T ratings
for the corresponding duct firestop system must also
be at least equivalent to the duct enclosure system
and the surrounding assembly. For HV AC ducts, the
stability, integrity and fire resistance rating of the duct
enclosure system must be at least equivalent to the
rating of the construction assembly penetrated.

Step One:
Firestopping Inspection Manual
International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Guidelines
Step Two:

Guidelines
Verify the field installation is consistent with the
parameters of the listing and therefore compliant.
A. Duct System Type – kitchen grease exhaust,
hazardous material exhaust, ventilation,
supply/ return, etc.
B. Duct Construction – dimensions, material,
gauge, reinforcement, connections, vertical or
horizontal orientation.
C. Enclosure System – labeled components,
number of layers, fire rating, required
clearance to combustibles, thickness and
density of material, material joints (overlap of
material, taping of cut edges or seams), etc.
D. Enclosure System Attachment – mechanical
method of attachment to duct (typically steel
banding and/or capacitor discharge insulation
pins), components, spacing, gauge, etc.
E. Duct Supports – hanger system components,
frequency of location, clearance to enclosure
system, protection requirements.
F. Access Door – field fabricated or prefabricated
door construction and protection with
enclosure system material must match design
listing.
G. Firestop System – refer to design listing for fire
rated assembly construction, annular space,
packing material type and depth, and firestop
material type and depth.

Firestopping Inspection Manual
Scope
• Construction documents detailing the fire-
stop locations and systems must be kept on
site to assist in the conduct of the inspection.
• Insure to a reasonable degree that empty
containers, wrappings or boxes of the speci-
fied materials are in sufficient quantity to
have been installed correctly.
• Insure to a reasonable degree that the actual
products, containers, wrappings or boxes
are labeled with the approved testing agency
marks and are as specified in the submitted
details.
• Measure the depth and width of materials as
indicated in the details (sometimes density
measurements are also required for products
such as thermal insulation).
• Insure to a reasonable degree that joints
have been installed in such manner that the
required movement can be achieved.
• Compare the installed firestop system with
the approved submitted details.
• Insure a reasonable degree of workmanship,
which would indicate compliance with the
specified design.

8 International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
The ability of penetration firestop systems,
fire resistive joint systems and ventilation
systems to perform their intended function of
fire containment is directly related to the quality
of their installations. Thorough inspection is an
integral component of any passive fire protection
quality control program. It is not realistic to
visually inspect each penetration and the entire
length of every joint and ventilation duct. How
many inspections are enough? This is a judgment
call by the inspector; however, the ASTM
inspection standards may be used as a guideline.
Field Inspection Process
Scope
Major elements of quality firestop
inspections are:
• Firestop systems must not be concealed from
view before being inspected and approved
(IBC 110.3.6).
• Walk through visual inspections should be
made during the rough and final inspections.
• When necessary or required, destructive
evaluation will be made on various types of
firestop systems.
• Appropriate tools for firestop inspections
should include a flashlight, measuring device
and cutting tool.
• Proper material depths, annular space, at-
tachments, spacing and product type are
critical to the effectiveness of the system.

Lorem Ipsum
7International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
Scope
The time allocated for inspections can be
drastically reduced if the proper paperwork is
provided on the approved plans. Planning and
communication between the building designer,
structural engineer and the installer prior to
construction will save time, costs and resources
in assuring the application of the proper systems.
Verification of system testing and listings
with a nationally recognized laboratory, prior
to installation in the field, is key to a smooth
inspection process. Use of applicable ASTM
practices will provide guidelines for inspection
of installed systems.
Inspection Process

ScopeEngineering Judgments
It is not unusual to find, in construction
projects, unique conditions which have not
been tested and listed, that require special
consideration. The protection of these conditions
will necessitate Engineering Judgments (EJ’s)
since they have not been tested and do not

Firestopping Inspection Manual
Scope
Construction codes have very clear require-
ments on passive fire protection. These require-
ments are included in Chapter 7 Fire and Smoke
Protection Features of the International Code
Council (ICC) International Building Code
© (IBC©).
Whenever required by the IBC©, the fire
resistance ratings of floors, walls, floor/ceiling,
roof/ceiling assemblies or fire-resistance-rated
duct enclosures must be restored when an open-
ing is made to accommodate penetrations for
mechanical, electrical, plumbing, communication
systems and ventilation ducts. joints between
floors, walls, floors and walls, etc, must also have
the same fire resistance ratings as the adjacent
construction.
NFPA 101 (Life Safety Code), NFPA 70 (National
Electric Code), the International Mechanical
Code
© (IMC©) and the International Plumbing
Code© (IPC©) also include provisions related to
the protection of penetrations. The codes have
explicit requirements for inspection of firestop
systems before they are concealed.
The IBC 2012 107.2.2 requires that evidence be
submitted to the building official showing that
the materials and methods of construction used
to protect penetrations, joints and ventilation
ducts in fire resistance rated building elements
shall not reduce the required fire resistance
rating. The International Fire Code
© has require-
ments for periodic inspection of firestop systems
throughout the life of the building.
Building Code Requirements

The following information is intended to
provide construction and code enforcement
professionals with basic checkpoints to ensure
that the required fire resistance ratings are
maintained when through penetrations and
linear joints breach walls and floors.

be all encompassing or to be used as a design
guide. It is for information and educational
purposes only.

The protection of these conditions will necessitate Engineering Judgments (EJ’s) since they have not been tested and do not comply with a published design listing. The International Firestop Council has published “Recommended IFC Guidelines for Evaluating Firestop Systems in Engineering Judgments” to assist designers, plan reviewers and inspectors in addressing nonconforming construction details. Planning and communication between the building designer, structural engineer and the installer prior to construction will save time, costs and resources in assuring the application of the proper systems. Verification of system testing and listings with a nationally recognized laboratory, prior to installation in the field, is key to a smooth inspection process. Use of applicable ASTM practices will provide guidelines for inspection of installed systems. Thorough inspection is an integral component of any passive fire protection quality control program. It is not realistic to visually inspect each penetration and the entire length of every joint and ventilation duct. How many inspections are enough? This is a judgment call by the inspector; however, the ASTM inspection standards may be used as a guideline. Field Inspection Process Scope Major elements of quality firestop inspections are: • Firestop systems must not be concealed from view before being inspected and approved (IBC 110.3.6). • Walk through visual inspections should be made during the rough and final inspections. • When necessary or required, destructive evaluation will be made on various types of firestop systems. • Appropriate tools for firestop inspections should include a flashlight, measuring device and cutting tool. • Proper material depths, annular space, at- tachments, spacing and product type are critical to the effectiveness of the system. Lorem Ipsum 9International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Firestopping Inspection Manual Scope • Construction documents detailing the fire- stop locations and systems must be kept on site to assist in the conduct of the inspection. • Insure to a reasonable degree that empty containers, wrappings or boxes of the speci- fied materials are in sufficient quantity to have been installed correctly. • Insure to a reasonable degree that the actual products, containers, wrappings or boxes are labeled with the approved testing agency marks and are as specified in the submitted details. • Measure the depth and width of materials as indicated in the details (sometimes density measurements are also required for products such as thermal insulation). • Insure to a reasonable degree that joints have been installed in such manner that the required movement can be achieved. • Compare the installed firestop system with the approved submitted details. • Insure a reasonable degree of workmanship, which would indicate compliance with the specified design. The code requires that the rating of a joint system shall not be less than the fire resistive ratings of the adjacent assemblies. Step Three: Verify the documents and submitted drawings reference systems that have been tested for the required amount of movement. A system listing a nominal 1 inch joint width with 25% compression or extension, actually allows for a movement of 1/4” of compression and 1/4” of extension. Verify the documents and submitted drawings have been reviewed by the Project Design Professional and/or the structural engineer and that they meet the allowable movement requirements. Verify the documents and submitted drawings reference tested and listed fire resistive joint systems tested to ASTM E1966 or UL 2079 by accredited testing laboratories or certified third party testing agencies. These systems should be published or readily available via the internet or other means. All other materials should not be used. Verify the rating of the system is greater than or equal to the rating of the floor. The continuity requirements within the building codes state that the rating of a floor assembly must extend to and be tight against an exterior wall. Verify documents and submitted drawings reference legitimate listed Perimeter Fire Barrier Systems. Documents referencing only fire resistive joint systems such as FF, FW or HW should not be accepted for curtain wall applications. For Perimeter Fire Barrier Systems (Curtain Walls) 15 Firestopping Inspection Manual International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Guidelines Verify insulation type and brand used is listed within the tested system. Mineral wool is the typical insulation of choice. If mineral wool is used it must be installed to the correct compression and according to the correct orientation. Verify documents reference systems that have been tested with windows or vision glass if the building has glazing close to the safing area. Some systems were tested with glazing close to the safing area while other systems were for structures with limited glazing such as storage and warehouse facilities. Verify a stiff steel reinforcement member, if required, has been placed behind exposed curtain wall panel insulation. Typical stiffening members can be steel hat channels, “L” or “T” angles. A common inspection practice is to be on site just prior to the addition of the sealant to verify the correct application thickness is being followed and to verify correct orientation of mineral wool. The inspector may request samples from the installing contractor after which the installing contractor shall make the necessary repairs to the destructively sampled area. A scale or caliper is sufficient for measuring the sealant depth. Step Nine: Verify safing clips or “Z” clips have been used if the system requires it. Verify that exposed mullions, if required by the system, are covered with the proper insulating barrier securely fastened with mechanical fasteners per the system design. Step Ten: Step Seven: If required by the tested system, verify insulation panels are securely fastened with mechanical fasteners per the listed system. Guidelines Lorem Ipsum 17International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Firestopping Inspection Manual As an overview of the above steps, verify the parameters indicated in the system are the same as those installed in the field: (Download checklist form from the IFC web site at www.firestop.org). Overview: Guidelines A. Is the perimeter fire barrier system tested for the type and nature of assembly, (minimum thickness of concrete, transom spacing, etc.)? B. Is the rating of the perimeter fire barrier system equal or greater than the floor assembly? C. Do the supplied products have labels from a recognized quality assurance agency? D. Does the field installation follow the listing? a. Width of gap between floor edge and curtain wall at time of installation. b. Design detail includes vision glass if applicable. c. Specified curtain wall spandrel insulation, (type, thickness, density, etc.). d. Specified spandrel panel perimeter angles, (gauge thickness, dimensions, fastener spacing). e. Specified framing and/or mullion covering, (type, thickness, density, etc). f. Support clips for safing insulation, if specified. g. Specified forming or safing insulation, (type, % compression, depth, etc). h. Specified sealant, coating, device or firestopping product, (type, depth, location). 16 Firestopping Inspection Manual International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Step Eight: Verify coating or sealant has been applied to the proper depth. A common inspection practice is to be on site just prior to the addition of the sealant to verify the correct application thickness is being followed and to verify correct orientation of mineral wool. The inspector may request samples from the installing contractor after which the installing contractor shall make the necessary repairs to the destructively sampled area. A scale or caliper is sufficient for measuring the sealant depth. Step Nine: Verify safing clips or “Z” clips have been used if the system requires it. Verify that exposed mullions, if required by the system, are covered with the proper insulating barrier securely fastened with mechanical fasteners per the system design. Step Ten: Step Seven: If required by the tested system, verify insulation panels are securely fastened with mechanical fasteners per the listed system. Guidelines 15 Firestopping Inspection Manual International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Guidelines Verify insulation type and brand used is listed within the tested system. Mineral wool is the typical insulation of choice. If mineral wool is used it must be installed to the correct compression and according to the correct orientation. Verify documents reference systems that have been tested with windows or vision glass if the building has glazing close to the safing area. Some systems were tested with glazing close to the safing area while other systems were for structures with limited glazing such as storage and warehouse facilities. Verify a stiff steel reinforcement member, if required, has been placed behind exposed curtain wall panel insulation. Typical stiffening members can be steel hat channels, “L” or “T” angles. Step Six: Step Four: Step Five: 18 Step One: Firestopping Inspection Manual International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Guidelines Step Three: Step Two: Verify the fire resistance rating of the duct enclosure system and corresponding firestop system are equal or greater than the required fire resistance ratings for the building construction assembly penetrated. For grease ducts, the IMC requires the fire resistance rating of the duct enclosure system be at least equivalent to the surrounding building Verify the duct enclosure system is tested to the appropriate Standard for the specific type of duct system. Grease duct enclosure systems are tested and listed per ASTM E2336, which includes a full scale ASTM E 119 engulfment test. HV AC duct enclosure systems are tested and listed per ISO 6944, Type A is for closed duct systems and Type B is for duct systems that contain openings. Verify the documents and submitted drawings reference legitimate fire resistive duct enclosure systems tested by accredited testing laboratories or certified third party testing agencies. These systems and insulation components should be listed, labeled, published and readily available via the internet or other means. For Fire-Resistance-Rated Duct Enclosure Systems 19 Firestopping Inspection Manual International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Guidelines Verify the field installation is consistent with the parameters of the listing and therefore compliant. A. Duct System Type – kitchen grease exhaust, hazardous material exhaust, ventilation, supply/ return, etc. B. Duct Construction – dimensions, material, gauge, reinforcement, connections, vertical or horizontal orientation. C. Enclosure System – labeled components, number of layers, fire rating, required clearance to combustibles, thickness and density of material, material joints (overlap of material, taping of cut edges or seams), etc. D. Enclosure System Attachment – mechanical method of attachment to duct (typically steel banding and/or capacitor discharge insulation pins), components, spacing, gauge, etc. E. Duct Supports – hanger system components, frequency of location, clearance to enclosure system, protection requirements. F. Access Door – field fabricated or prefabricated door construction and protection with enclosure system material must match design listing. G. Firestop System – refer to design listing for fire rated assembly construction, annular space, packing material type and depth, and firestop material type and depth. Step Four: construction assembly penetrated. The F and T ratings for the corresponding duct firestop system must also be at least equivalent to the duct enclosure system and the surrounding assembly. For HV AC ducts, the stability, integrity and fire resistance rating of the duct enclosure system must be at least equivalent to the rating of the construction assembly penetrated. 14 Step One: Firestopping Inspection Manual International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Guidelines Step Two: Step Three: Verify that the firestop material to be used is classified and listed for use in Perimeter Fire Barrier Systems. All other materials should not be used. Verify the rating of the system is greater than or equal to the rating of the floor. The continuity requirements within the building codes state that the rating of a floor assembly must extend to and be tight against an exterior wall. Verify documents and submitted drawings reference legitimate listed Perimeter Fire Barrier Systems. Documents referencing only fire resistive joint systems such as FF, FW or HW should not be accepted for curtain wall applications. For Perimeter Fire Barrier Systems (Curtain Walls) 13 Firestopping Inspection Manual International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 A. Is the joint system tested and listed? B. Is the joint system tested for the amount of movement required? C. Is the joint system tested for the class and type of movement required? D. Is the fire rating of the joint equal to (or greater than) the assemblies it is adjacent to? E. Observe the nominal installed width of the joint at the framing inspection. F. If a mechanical system is used, are the specified tracks installed with a third party testing agency label attached? G. Do the supplied products have labels from recognized quality assurance agency? H. Does the field installation follow the listing? a. Specified forming, packing or backing material? b. Specified type of sealant, coating or device? c. Specified amount, depth, location of sealant, coating or device? d. Specified accessory items – cover plates, bond breaker tape, and specified deflection track? Overview: Guidelines As an overview of these steps, verify the parameters indicated in the system are the same as those installed in the field: (Download checklist form at the IFC web site www.firestop.org). 20 Firestopping Inspection Manual International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Test Standards relevant to Firestop Systems: 1. ASTM E 814 (ANSI/UL 1479) “Standard Test Method for Fire Tests of Through-Penetration Fire Stops” 2. ASTM E 1966 (ANSI/UL 2079) “Standards Test Method for Fire-Resistive Joint Systems” 3. ASTM E 1399 “Cyclic Movement and Measuring the Minimum and Maximum Joint Widths of Architectural Joint Systems” 4. ASTM E 2174 “Standard Practice for On-Site Inspection of Installed Fire Stops” 5. ASTM E 2307 “Standard Test Method for Determining the Fire Resistance of Perimeter Fire Barrier Systems Using the Intermediate Scale, Multi-Story Test Apparatus” 6. ASTM E 2393 “Standard Practice for On-Site Inspection of Installed Fire Resistive Joint System and Perimeter Fire Barriers” 7. ASTM E 2336 “Standard Test Methods for Fire Resistive Grease Duct Enclosure Systems” 8. ISO 6944 “Fire Resistance Tests – Ventilation Ducts” 9. ICC ES AC179 “ Acceptance Criteria for Metallic HV AC Duct Enclosure Assemblies” 10. ASTM E-2750 “Standard Guide for Extension of Data from Firestop Penetration System Tests Conducted in Accordance with ASTM E814” Plan Review And Inspection Guidelines For Firestop Systems Applicable Standards: Guidelines 21 Firestopping Inspection Manual International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 There are several independent testing laboratories, also referred to as third party testing agencies, which conduct the fire testing of firestop, perimeter fire barrier and duct enclosure systems. The fire test results are usually included as design listings in the fire resistance directories published by the testing laboratory. These Directories are an important source of information during the plan review process and inspection process. The following are some of the recognized independent laboratories conducting tests of firestop systems: 1. Underwriters Laboratories Inc. Northbrook, IL (847) 272-8800 www.ul.com 2. Southwest Research Institute San Antonio, TX (210) 522-2311 www.fire.swri.org 3. Factory Mutual Norwood, MA (781) 762-4300 www.fmglobal.com 4. Intertek Testing Services San Antonio, TX (210) 625-8100 www.intertek.com Directories Third Party Testing Agencies: Independent Testing Laboratories 12 Step One: Firestopping Inspection Manual International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Guidelines Step Two: Verify the rating of the joint system is equal to the rating of the assemblies it is connecting. The code requires that the rating of a joint system shall not be less than the fire resistive ratings of the adjacent assemblies. Step Three: Verify the documents and submitted drawings reference systems that have been tested for the required amount of movement. A system listing a nominal 1 inch joint width with 25% compression or extension, actually allows for a movement of 1/4” of compression and 1/4” of extension. Verify the documents and submitted drawings have been reviewed by the Project Design Professional and/or the structural engineer and that they meet the allowable movement requirements. Verify the documents and submitted drawings reference tested and listed fire resistive joint systems tested to ASTM E1966 or UL 2079 by accredited testing laboratories or certified third party testing agencies. These systems should be published or readily available via the internet or other means. For Fire Resistive Joint Systems: Including Mechanical Fire Barrier Systems Step Four: 11 Firestopping Inspection Manual International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 A. Is the rating of the through penetration system equal or greater than the assembly penetrated? B. Do the supplied products have labels from a recognized quality assurance agency? C. Does the field installation follow the listing? a. For the size of opening prior to firestopping? b. For pipe conditions: Nature and quantity of penetrant(s), (material, size, diameter, insulation type & thickness, etc.)? For cable conditions: Allowable cable sizes, jacketing, spacing and bundle size or percent fill of opening (as listed)? c. Annular space requirements, (minimum, maximum, nominal, etc.)? d. Specified forming, packing or backing material, (when required)? e. Specified sealant, coating, device or firestopping product indicated, (type, amount, depth, location, etc.)? f. Specified accessory items, (anchors, fasteners, securing devices, plates, etc.)? These systems should be published and readily available via the internet or other means. Verify that the Through-Penetration System being used has been tested to the hourly rating necessary (ie. 1 hr., 2 hr., etc,) based on the type of assembly being penetrated. As an overview of these steps, verify that the parameters indicated in the system are the same as those installed in the field: Is the through penetration system rated for the type and nature of assembly (thickness of concrete, stud width, etc.)? For Penetration Firestop Systems Lorem Ipsum 9International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Firestopping Inspection Manual Scope • Construction documents detailing the fire- stop locations and systems must be kept on site to assist in the conduct of the inspection. • Insure to a reasonable degree that empty containers, wrappings or boxes of the speci- fied materials are in sufficient quantity to have been installed correctly. • Insure to a reasonable degree that the actual products, containers, wrappings or boxes are labeled with the approved testing agency marks and are as specified in the submitted details. • Measure the depth and width of materials as indicated in the details (sometimes density measurements are also required for products such as thermal insulation). • Insure to a reasonable degree that joints have been installed in such manner that the required movement can be achieved. • Compare the installed firestop system with the approved submitted details. • Insure a reasonable degree of workmanship, which would indicate compliance with the specified design. www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Firestopping Inspection Manual The ability of penetration firestop systems, fire resistive joint systems and ventilation systems to perform their intended function of fire containment is directly related to the quality of their installations. Thorough inspection is an integral component of any passive fire protection quality control program. It is not realistic to visually inspect each penetration and the entire length of every joint and ventilation duct. How many inspections are enough? This is a judgment call by the inspector; however, the ASTM inspection standards may be used as a guideline. Field Inspection Process Scope Major elements of quality firestop inspections are: • Firestop systems must not be concealed from view before being inspected and approved (IBC 110.3.6). • Walk through visual inspections should be made during the rough and final inspections. • When necessary or required, destructive evaluation will be made on various types of firestop systems. • Appropriate tools for firestop inspections should include a flashlight, measuring device and cutting tool. • Proper material depths, annular space, at- tachments, spacing and product type are critical to the effectiveness of the system. Lorem Ipsum 7International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Firestopping Inspection Manual Scope The time allocated for inspections can be drastically reduced if the proper paperwork is provided on the approved plans. Planning and communication between the building designer, structural engineer and the installer prior to construction will save time, costs and resources in assuring the application of the proper systems. Verification of system testing and listings with a nationally recognized laboratory, prior to installation in the field, is key to a smooth inspection process. Use of applicable ASTM practices will provide guidelines for inspection of installed systems. Inspection Process Lorem Ipsum 6 International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Firestopping Inspection Manual ScopeEngineering Judgments It is not unusual to find, in construction projects, unique conditions which have not been tested and listed, that require special consideration. The protection of these conditions will necessitate Engineering Judgments (EJ’s) since they have not been tested and do not comply with a published design listing. The International Firestop Council has published “Recommended IFC Guidelines for Evaluating Firestop Systems in Engineering Judgments” to assist designers, plan reviewers and inspectors in addressing nonconforming construction details. The plan submittals should always indicate which details are based on EJ’s. The submitted EJ’s need to be approved by the building official, and made available to the field inspector when approved. The IFC guidelines for the evaluation of EJ’s can be obtained from the IFC website: www.firestop.org Lorem Ipsum 5International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Firestopping Inspection Manual Scope The authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) must review and approve firestop system details and rated ducted enclosures. Hence, firestop systems details and materials must be included on the plans and specifications. Manufacturer’s cut sheets are often accepted if they are generated by an approved testing agency. If details, products and specifications are not sufficient to provide clear directions to the general contractor and firestop installer, the submittals should be noted as incomplete and returned to the designer to be resubmitted with the required information. If the plans and specifications are clear and complete, most field problems with firestop systems can be avoided. Plans Examination / Review Lorem Ipsum 28 International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Firestopping Inspection Manual Firestopping GuidelinesAbout the International Code Council The International Code Council is a member-fo- cused association dedicated to helping the build- ing safety community and construction industry provide safe, sustainable and affordable construc- tion through the development of codes and stan- dards used in the design, build and compliance process. Most U.S. communities and many global markets choose the International Codes. ICC Evaluation Service (ICC-ES), a subsidiary of the International Code Council, has been the industry leader in performing technical evaluations for code compliance fostering safe and sustainable design and construction. Headquarters: 500 New Jersey Avenue, NW, 6th Floor, Washington, DC 20001-2070 District Offices: Birmingham, AL; Chicago. IL; Los Angeles, CA 1-888-422-7233 www.iccsafe.org Lorem Ipsum 29International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Firestopping Inspection Manual Firestopping GuidelinesNotes: Lorem Ipsum 4 International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769 Firestopping Inspection Manual Scope Construction codes have very clear require- ments on passive fire protection. These require- ments are included in Chapter 7 Fire and Smoke Protection Features of the International Code Council (ICC) International Building Code © (IBC©). Whenever required by the IBC©, the fire resistance ratings of floors, walls, floor/ceiling, roof/ceiling assemblies or fire-resistance-rated duct enclosures must be restored when an open- ing is made to accommodate penetrations for mechanical, electrical, plumbing, communication systems and ventilation ducts. joints between floors, walls, floors and walls, etc, must also have the same fire resistance ratings as the adjacent construction. NFPA 101 (Life Safety Code), NFPA 70 (National Electric Code), the International Mechanical Code © (IMC©) and the International Plumbing Code© (IPC©) also include provisions related to the protection of penetrations. The codes have explicit requirements for inspection of firestop systems before they are concealed. The IBC 2012 107.2.2 requires that evidence be submitted to the building official showing that the materials and methods of construction used to protect penetrations, joints and ventilation ducts in fire resistance rated building elements shall not reduce the required fire resistance rating. The International Fire Code © has require- ments for periodic inspection of firestop systems throughout the life of the building. This inspection guideline is not intended to be all encompassing or to be used as a design guide. It is for information and educational purposes only. The Source of Firestop Expertise SM

construction.

© (IMC©) and the International Plumbing

Firestopping Inspection Manual
ScopeEngineering Judgments
It is not unusual to find, in construction
projects, unique conditions which have not
been tested and listed, that require special
consideration. The protection of these conditions
will necessitate Engineering Judgments (EJ’s)
since they have not been tested and do not
comply with a published design listing.
The International Firestop Council has
published “Recommended IFC Guidelines for
Evaluating Firestop Systems in Engineering
Judgments” to assist designers, plan reviewers
and inspectors in addressing nonconforming
construction details. The plan submittals should
always indicate which details are based on EJ’s.
The submitted EJ’s need to be approved by the
building official, and made available to the field
inspector when approved.
The IFC guidelines for the evaluation of EJ’s can be
obtained from the IFC website: www.firestop.org

Firestopping Inspection Manual
Scope
The time allocated for inspections can be
drastically reduced if the proper paperwork is
provided on the approved plans. Planning and
communication between the building designer,
structural engineer and the installer prior to
construction will save time, costs and resources
in assuring the application of the proper systems.
Verification of system testing and listings
with a nationally recognized laboratory, prior
to installation in the field, is key to a smooth
inspection process. Use of applicable ASTM
practices will provide guidelines for inspection
of installed systems.

8 International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
The ability of penetration firestop systems,
fire resistive joint systems and ventilation
systems to perform their intended function of
fire containment is directly related to the quality
of their installations. Thorough inspection is an
integral component of any passive fire protection
quality control program. It is not realistic to
visually inspect each penetration and the entire
length of every joint and ventilation duct. How
many inspections are enough? This is a judgment
call by the inspector; however, the ASTM
inspection standards may be used as a guideline.
Field Inspection Process
Scope
Major elements of quality firestop
inspections are:
• Firestop systems must not be concealed from
view before being inspected and approved
(IBC 110.3.6).
• Walk through visual inspections should be
made during the rough and final inspections.
• When necessary or required, destructive
evaluation will be made on various types of
firestop systems.
• Appropriate tools for firestop inspections
should include a flashlight, measuring device
and cutting tool.
• Proper material depths, annular space, at-
tachments, spacing and product type are
critical to the effectiveness of the system.

9International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
Scope
• Construction documents detailing the fire-
stop locations and systems must be kept on
site to assist in the conduct of the inspection.
• Insure to a reasonable degree that empty
containers, wrappings or boxes of the speci-
fied materials are in sufficient quantity to
have been installed correctly.
• Insure to a reasonable degree that the actual
products, containers, wrappings or boxes
are labeled with the approved testing agency
marks and are as specified in the submitted
details.
• Measure the depth and width of materials as
indicated in the details (sometimes density
measurements are also required for products
such as thermal insulation).
• Insure to a reasonable degree that joints
have been installed in such manner that the
required movement can be achieved.
• Compare the installed firestop system with
the approved submitted details.
• Insure a reasonable degree of workmanship,
which would indicate compliance with the
specified design.

Guidelines
Step Two:
Verify the rating of the joint system is equal to the
rating of the assemblies it is connecting. The code
requires that the rating of a joint system shall not
be less than the fire resistive ratings of the adjacent
assemblies.
Step Three:
Verify the documents and submitted drawings
reference systems that have been tested for the
required amount of movement. A system listing a
nominal 1 inch joint width with 25% compression or
extension, actually allows for a movement of 1/4” of
compression and 1/4” of extension.
Verify the documents and submitted drawings have
been reviewed by the Project Design Professional
and/or the structural engineer and that they meet the
allowable movement requirements.
Verify the documents and submitted drawings
reference tested and listed fire resistive joint systems
tested to ASTM E1966 or UL 2079 by accredited testing
laboratories or certified third party testing agencies.
These systems should be published or readily available
via the internet or other means.

Barrier Systems Using the Intermediate Scale,
Multi-Story Test Apparatus”
6. ASTM E 2393 “Standard Practice for On-Site

and Perimeter Fire Barriers”
7. ASTM E 2336 “Standard Test Methods for Fire
Resistive Grease Duct Enclosure Systems”

Applicable Standards:
Guidelines

19
Firestopping Inspection Manual
International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Guidelines
Verify the field installation is consistent with the
parameters of the listing and therefore compliant.
A. Duct System Type – kitchen grease exhaust,
hazardous material exhaust, ventilation,
supply/ return, etc.
B. Duct Construction – dimensions, material,
gauge, reinforcement, connections, vertical or
horizontal orientation.
C. Enclosure System – labeled components,
number of layers, fire rating, required
clearance to combustibles, thickness and
density of material, material joints (overlap of
material, taping of cut edges or seams), etc.
D. Enclosure System Attachment – mechanical
method of attachment to duct (typically steel
banding and/or capacitor discharge insulation
pins), components, spacing, gauge, etc.
E. Duct Supports – hanger system components,
frequency of location, clearance to enclosure
system, protection requirements.
F. Access Door – field fabricated or prefabricated
door construction and protection with
enclosure system material must match design
listing.
G. Firestop System – refer to design listing for fire
rated assembly construction, annular space,
packing material type and depth, and firestop
material type and depth.

Verify insulation type and brand used is
listed within the tested system. Mineral wool
is the typical insulation of choice. If mineral
wool is used it must be installed to the correct
compression and according to the correct
orientation.
Verify documents reference systems that have
been tested with windows or vision glass if the
building has glazing close to the safing area.
Some systems were tested with glazing close
to the safing area while other systems were for
structures with limited glazing such as storage
and warehouse facilities.
Verify a stiff steel reinforcement member, if
required, has been placed behind exposed curtain
wall panel insulation. Typical stiffening members
can be steel hat channels, “L” or “T” angles.

Step Eight:
Verify coating or sealant has been applied to the
proper depth. A common inspection practice is to be
on site just prior to the addition of the sealant to verify
the correct application thickness is being followed
and to verify correct orientation of mineral wool. The
inspector may request samples from the installing
contractor after which the installing contractor shall
make the necessary repairs to the destructively
sampled area. A scale or caliper is sufficient for
measuring the sealant depth.

Verify safing clips or “Z” clips have been used if the
system requires it.

system, are covered with the proper insulating barrier
securely fastened with mechanical fasteners per the
system design.

Guidelines
Verify the field installation is consistent with the
parameters of the listing and therefore compliant.
A. Duct System Type – kitchen grease exhaust,
hazardous material exhaust, ventilation,
supply/ return, etc.
B. Duct Construction – dimensions, material,
gauge, reinforcement, connections, vertical or
horizontal orientation.
C. Enclosure System – labeled components,
number of layers, fire rating, required
clearance to combustibles, thickness and
density of material, material joints (overlap of
material, taping of cut edges or seams), etc.
D. Enclosure System Attachment – mechanical
method of attachment to duct (typically steel
banding and/or capacitor discharge insulation
pins), components, spacing, gauge, etc.
E. Duct Supports – hanger system components,
frequency of location, clearance to enclosure
system, protection requirements.
F. Access Door – field fabricated or prefabricated
door construction and protection with
enclosure system material must match design
listing.
G. Firestop System – refer to design listing for fire
rated assembly construction, annular space,
packing material type and depth, and firestop
material type and depth.

and the surrounding assembly. For HV AC ducts, the
stability, integrity and fire resistance rating of the duct
enclosure system must be at least equivalent to the
rating of the construction assembly penetrated.

Firestopping Inspection Manual
Scope
• Construction documents detailing the fire-
stop locations and systems must be kept on
site to assist in the conduct of the inspection.
• Insure to a reasonable degree that empty
containers, wrappings or boxes of the speci-
fied materials are in sufficient quantity to
have been installed correctly.
• Insure to a reasonable degree that the actual
products, containers, wrappings or boxes
are labeled with the approved testing agency
marks and are as specified in the submitted
details.
• Measure the depth and width of materials as
indicated in the details (sometimes density
measurements are also required for products
such as thermal insulation).
• Insure to a reasonable degree that joints
have been installed in such manner that the
required movement can be achieved.
• Compare the installed firestop system with
the approved submitted details.
• Insure a reasonable degree of workmanship,
which would indicate compliance with the
specified design.

8 International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
The ability of penetration firestop systems,
fire resistive joint systems and ventilation
systems to perform their intended function of
fire containment is directly related to the quality
of their installations. Thorough inspection is an
integral component of any passive fire protection
quality control program. It is not realistic to
visually inspect each penetration and the entire
length of every joint and ventilation duct. How
many inspections are enough? This is a judgment
call by the inspector; however, the ASTM
inspection standards may be used as a guideline.
Field Inspection Process
Scope
Major elements of quality firestop
inspections are:
• Firestop systems must not be concealed from
view before being inspected and approved
(IBC 110.3.6).
• Walk through visual inspections should be
made during the rough and final inspections.
• When necessary or required, destructive
evaluation will be made on various types of
firestop systems.
• Appropriate tools for firestop inspections
should include a flashlight, measuring device
and cutting tool.
• Proper material depths, annular space, at-
tachments, spacing and product type are
critical to the effectiveness of the system.

Lorem Ipsum
7International FireStop Council - www.firestop.org - 877.241.3769
Firestopping Inspection Manual
Scope
The time allocated for inspections can be
drastically reduced if the proper paperwork is
provided on the approved plans. Planning and
communication between the building designer,
structural engineer and the installer prior to
construction will save time, costs and resources
in assuring the application of the proper systems.
Verification of system testing and listings
with a nationally recognized laboratory, prior
to installation in the field, is key to a smooth
inspection process. Use of applicable ASTM
practices will provide guidelines for inspection
of installed systems.
Inspection Process
